title: Ah. We’re sorry you’re having trouble with that page
summary: Family tree pages are viewable only by Mueller descendants. If you have a place on the Mueller family tree — as direct descendant, as spouse, or as a kindred spirit — your username and password should let you move around the site freely and read whatever you like. Those credentials are easily and quickly available to Mueller descendants.
content:
You will receive your username and password, usually within 24 hours. When you have them, go to [www.frauerica.org](https://www.frauerica.org) and enter them in the spaces provided. As long as you allow your browser to set and keep a cookie, the web will recognize your place on the Mueller family tree and you will not need to log in again.

FrauErica.org is built around a graphical representation of the family tree, beginning with Georg and Gertrude Mueller in Napoleanic times. Lots of other families plug in as direct descendants — Kniefs, Oltroges, Schumms, Nickels, Marquardts, Gramses, Feiertags, Rehwinkels — a *Who’s Who* of old Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod names. (C.F.W. Walther himself even has a small walk-on part.) **All are welcome.**

Less than a year after their arrival in America, the Muellers were swindled out of almost everything they owned by a dishonest land agent and a co-conspiritor who may been a Lutheran minister. As American life and commerce moved online, so did the digital counterparts of the land agent who bilked Fritz Mueller.

FrauErica.org tries to take reasonable precautions. Our first line of defense is not to include personal contact information or anything of material value online. We do not sell anything, and we do not ask for money. We print names and relationships, but we do not print addresses, phone numbers, e-mail addresses or any contact information — and we will never give anyone’s information to a third party. There is, however, very little in these pages that cannot be gotten elsewhere with a few clicks of the mouse.

We set cookies only to identify you as a Mueller descendant and to help you find your way around on the family tree. Explore to your heart’s content — we will not track your movements or gather any data on your usage, and absolutely nothing we have will be given or sold to anyone.

How much security is enough? You would probably want something a little more robust for your online banking, but FrauErica.org’s precautions should be enough to keep hobby hackers and automated web crawlers at bay. Our Family Tree pages are password-protected and are open only to people who have a place somewhere on the Mueller family tree. Many of our text pages are open to the public, though they do not contain much that is of value beyond the Mueller family.

Our site security is in your hands. Please do not share your username and password. If you know other Mueller friends and relations who need access to the site, we will happily provide a username and password to anyone on the Family Tree no matter how distant the branch. Please ask them to contact the Archivist as above.

The idea was to construct a dependable family history as a series of web pages, allowing users to click backward and forward through the generations, following different family lines. The data points for such a project quickly mounted into the tens of thousands — variant spellings, differing dates for births or deaths, Anglicized German names, and the whole matter of German translation — but at least there is data for the family tree pages.

The text files are a bit more delicate, since subjective elements inevitably creep in. If you encounter something with which you disagree or something you know is not quite right, *please* [Contact the Archivist](https://www.frauerica.org/Format/Contact.php?who=archivist).

title: A User’s Guide to FrauErica.org
summary: FrauErica.org is built around a graphical representation of the family tree, beginning with Georg and Gertrude Mueller in Napoleanic times. Lots of other families plug in — Kniefs, Oltroges, Schumms, Nickels, Marquardts, Gramses, Feiertags, Rehwinkels and many more, a *Who’s Who* of old Lutheran Church – Missouri Synod names. (C.F.W. Walther himself even has a small walk-on role.) All of the photographs, journals, letters, newspaper clippings, obituaries and so forth are in files that are clickable from the family tree. Various family archives have lots more. It seemed easiest just to post it all to the Web.
content:
{{image:3000}}

Each page of the family tree can have information on three generations. The middle generation — the yellow boxes, right — typically contains a married couple, though people who never married are represented there as well. The grandparents — parents of the yellow-box people — are above them in lilac-colored boxes, one pair of parents above each person in the middle generation. Children, if any, are below in green boxes.

Clickable orange triangles are the key to navigation. They appear in a name box when the archives has more material about the person’s family tree.

![](../Format/G1D.gif)Click on an upward-pointing orange triangle in the grandparents generation, and those two grandparents become the middle generation of a new page. Their parents will appear in the grandparent boxes. This is how you can work your way “north” through earlier generations, choosing to follow different branches, different sets of in-laws.

![](../Format/G2O.gif)An orange sideways-pointing triangle in the middle generation indicates a previous or subsequent marriage. Click to read a page with the other spouse, the children from that marriage, and information on a different branch of the family.

![](../Format/G3U.gif)Click on a downward-pointing orange triangle in one of the children’s boxes, and a new page will be displayed with that child in the middle generation. The child’s parents will become grandparents. A spouse may appear, perhaps a new set of children. This is how you can work your way “south” from earlier generations to where you are.

![](../Format/G3G.gif)When you log in for the first time, the website should automatically mark your own line of descent from Georg and Gertrude or other forebears to your branch of the family tree. Your line will be marked by a cluster of small orange diamonds rather than the orange triangle. This is handy for finding your way through 19th-century ancestors who may have had eight siblings and eight or nine children of their own.

**An emerging new feature:**  
Beginning in the spring of 2025, a new “Ancestry” navigation category began appearing on select home pages. Generally, the parental surnames of people in the yellow “parent generation” boxes may appear there. Clicking on one will take you to the first-generation page of that family, allowing you to click down through the generations via the small orange diamond cluster mentioned above. While any user may click on an ancestral name, the diamond clusters will only appear for direct descendants of earlier generations. This feature will gradually appear on more home pages.

If it‘s been awhile since you first logged in, it’s possible that the archive now has more information and more people in the lines of descent. To update your settings, click the [Update your account](https://www.frauerica.org/Format/UpdateAccount.php) in the navigation column, type in your username and password, and you’ll have the latest. If there is some information you are expecting but do not see, please [contact the archivist](https://www.frauerica.org/Format/Contact_Text.php).

{{image:3}}

The Frau Erica site has lots of other narrative materials — photographs, journals, obituaries, scanned documents, translations, essays, maps, even recipes — that are accessible from Family Tree pages. These document files have a slightly different graphic format — most noticeably a vertical orange bar to the left of the text.

At least two of the following commands will appear in the navigation column of every file on the Frau Erica site:

**User’s Guide** will take you to these explanatory pages.

**Ur-Muellers** will take you to the topmost page on the Mueller Family Tree: Georg and Gertrude.

**Index of Persons** contains links to the appropriate family page for everyone who appears anywhere. Very handy if successive clicks get you lost in the archives.

**Index of Texts** offers links to larger textual works — journals, autobiographies, newspaper stories, family histories, interviews and so forth.

**Photo Galleries** gathers and groups images that would otherwise be scattered throughout the site.

**Mueller Lexicon** produces a growing list of favorite Mueller German words and phrases: *Pech, Kaplier, Bockhans* and many others.

**Back to Family Page** appears when you have left the Family Tree. It will return you to the last Family Tree page you were reading, no matter how many other pages you have visited.

**My Home Page** will take you to the page designated as your home page. (FrauErica.org will also open to your home page if you have designated one.)

**Contact the Archivist** appears on nearly all pages, inviting you to email the Archivist with comments, additions, or corrections.

We do not need money. What we need is dependable information — dates, facts, figures, photographs, letters, certificates of all kinds (birth, baptism, confirmation, wedding, death, military induction, citizenship, mortgage, obituary, newspaper clippings and so on) — about anyone who is connected to the Mueller family by blood, by marriage, by adoption, or however. The more we get, the better the site becomes.

If you have materials from your branch of the Mueller family and would like to offer them to the Frau Erica Project, [Contact the Archivist](https://www.frauerica.org/Format/Contact_Text.php) and describe who you are, what you have, and how it fits in. One word of caution: Archives are precious and irreplaceable; keep your originals. Send only copies — in high-resolution digital form if at all possible. The Archivist can help.

The idea was to construct a dependable family history as a series of web pages, allowing users to click backward and forward through the generations, following different family lines. The data points for such a project quickly mounted into the thousands with attendant difficulties — variant spellings, differing dates for births or deaths, Anglicized names, and the whole matter of German translation — but at least there is data.

The text files are a bit more delicate, since subjective judgments inevitably creep in. If you encounter something with which you disagree or something you know is not quite right, *please* [Contact the Archivist](https://www.frauerica.org/Format/Contact_Text.php).

The Muellers began their life in America by losing nearly everything they owned in a land swindle. One scam like that should be enough for several generations.

As American life and commerce moved online, so did the digital counterparts of the land agent who bilked Fritz Mueller. FrauErica.org tries to take reasonable precautions; it pays to be careful. Our first line of defense is not to include personal contact information or anything of material value online. We print names and relationships, but we do not print addresses, phone numbers, e-mail addresses or any contact information — and we will never give anyone’s information to a third party. There is, however, very little in these pages that cannot be gotten elsewhere with a few clicks of the mouse.

How much security is enough? You would probably want something a little more robust for your online banking, but FrauErica.org’s precautions should be enough to keep hobby hackers and automated web crawlers at bay. Our Family Tree pages are password-protected and are open only to people who have a position somewhere on the Mueller family tree. Many of our text pages are open to the public, though they do not contain much that is of value beyond the Mueller family.

Our site security is in your hands. Please do not share your userID and password. If you know other Mueller relatives who would like to have access to the site, we will happily provide a userID and password no matter how distant the connection. Please ask them to [Contact the Archivist](https://www.frauerica.org/Format/Contact_Text.php).

title: Adelheid Erica Amanda Mueller Nickel
summary: This brief biography was read during Adelheid’s memorial service at Calvary Lutheran Church in Stayton, Oregon.
content:
{{image:5}}

Adelheid Erica Amanda Mueller Nickel was born in Madison, Minnesota, June 8, 1907. She was the fourth of Ernst and Helen Mueller’s nine children and, as she often pointed out, was thought to be the most delicate, the least likely to survive childhood. She died July 13, 1999, at age 92, with four children, eight grandchildren, and one great-grandchild.

Adelheid was the daughter of a Lutheran minister in a German-speaking community near Janesville, Minnesota. She learned her catechism in German, worshipped in German, and spoke German with friends and family — at least until World War I made it unwise to speak German outside the home. She was born Lutheran, raised Lutheran and was thoroughly Lutheran all her life — which meant, among other things, that Adelheid could handle contradictions.

She knew how important it was to keep up appearances. As a minister’s child, she worried that members of her father’s congregation would not approve of something she or her siblings said or did. So she behaved properly at all times ... and then again, she pushed a few limits. As a child, Adelheid scandalized the Ladies Aid by singing and dancing to that most sinful of songs, “Yes, We Have No Bananas.” It was Adelheid who was the first to wear bloomers, the risqué new fashion for active women. (Adelheid played basketball at college during the Roaring Twenties. She was considered tall enough to be a center.)

She was raised to have a deep, conservative respect for tradition, yet it was Adelheid who brought new, cosmopolitan, forward-thinking notions from the larger world back to southern Minnesota. Among other innovations, she liberated families from the tyranny of salted, dried or canned meat by showing women how to prepare chop suey. She knew books, she knew music, she knew about modern things.

{{image:12}}

Adelheid attended Doctor Martin Luther College in New Ulm, Minnesota. At the time, women had few career choices, but that suited Adelheid just fine because one of them was teaching, and teaching was to become one of the great loves of her life. Better yet, young women who served as teachers in Lutheran schools were also expected to serve as organists in Lutheran congregations, and that suited Adelheid just fine as well. She graduated in 1929
[see page 58 of the [DMLC catalog](https://issuu.com/mlc.edu/docs/1930-1931_dmlc_catalog)]
and moved to La Crosse, Wisconsin, to begin her first teaching assignment. It was a tough placement in a poor community during the Great Depression, and Adelheid discovered that many children did not share her respect for authority. But she excelled and even managed to win over the town bully, a terrifying fourth grader named Billy, about whom she had been warned.

From there, Adelheid [was called to Milwaukee](AddieCall.php) as a teacher, organist and choir director at St. Lucas Church, a large Wisconsin Synod parish. Young women did not live in their own apartments in those days, so Adelheid arranged to live with a family a few blocks from the church. Albert and Margaret Nickel, her landlords, had a son exactly Adelheid’s age. Hilbert Clarence Eduard Nickel had also been born on June 8, 1907. Like Adelheid, he spoke German, attended school in German through the fourth grade, played the piano (mostly ragtime and popular music), and knew books. He made it his business to sing in Adelheid’s church choir and caught her attention by reciting Shakespeare. He even gave up his ragtime for Bach. They were married August 29, 1937, and settled in Oak Park, on the west side of Chicago.

Adelheid left her teaching career to raise four boys, but could not put aside her devotion to the classroom. Her passion for children’s literature brought piles of books into her home. Her excitement over the Newberry and Caldecott prizes for children’s books became an annual ritual. (Adelheid cultivated sources in the book department at Marshall Fields in the Loop, who sometimes told her which books were the winners before the prizes were publicly announced.) Art materials were everywhere — tempera paints, finger paints, modeling clay, papier maché. She invested in model railroads, piano lessons, recorder consorts, trips to the Art Institute, days at the zoo, and “surprise journeys,” and she organized family picnics on the lakefront or farther west in Elmhurst — for most years relying on public transportation. She was a Cubs fan, sat through countless Little League games, joined the booster club at Walther Lutheran High School, played a little tennis in her time, swam, fished and gardened.

{{image:159}}

She loved “fuschering,” which, as far as anyone knows, was a German term for exploring the woodlands, digging up interesting species of wildflowers and transplanting them to her backyard. There was wild ginger, Jack-in-the-Pulpit, three kinds of trillium, mandrake, even asparagus — all of it growing in a small patch of shaded ground in her yard. She harvested ferns during a camping trip in Maine and kept them alive in the car all the way back to Chicago. When she visited the Ivy House, her ancestral home in Germany, she returned through U.S. Customs with her blouse stuffed full of ancestral ivy, which now grows along a garage wall in Portland. Her famous sidelight was fuschering for heart-shaped rocks.

Adelheid’s life changed dramatically in 1966 when Hilbert died. After nearly 30 years of marriage, Adelheid was on her own again. She turned, inevitably, to the classroom — first near her home in Oak Park, then in the first grade at Zion Lutheran School in Bensenville, about 45 minutes away. She plunged in. Her young students didn’t just learn to read. They learned about authors and knew all about the Caldecott Awards. They painted Jack-in-the-Pulpits, learned to recognize birds, built teepees, and took field trips to the forest preserve. Every child finds a wonderful, memorable teacher on the way through grade school. For more than 10 classes at Zion Bensenville, Adelheid was that teacher.

{{image:155}}

She retired from teaching in 1981 and moved west to Portland, settling with her children’s books in the Ione Plaza Apartments. She now fuschered along the Pacific Coast, around Mt. Hood, down the Columbia Gorge, and on the banks of Panther Creek. She was a mother, grandmother, friend, church member, host for Sunday brunches and a prolific correspondent. As her health began to fail, she moved to the Marion Estates in Sublimity, reviewing and talking — now in English, now in German — about a long and eventful life devoted to children, church, family and friends.

*Selig sind die Toten, die in dem Herrn sterben. Ja, der Geist spricht. Sie ruhen von Ihren Arbeit und Ihrer Werke folgen Ihnen nach.*

title: The Call to St. Lucas Evangelical Lutheran Church
summary: In early October 1930, Adelheid Mueller received a formal call from St. Lucas Evangelical Lutheran Church, a Wisconsin-Synod congregation in Milwaukee. It was dated September 12, postmarked September 28. She was 23 at the time and had been teaching school in La Crosse, Wisconsin — her first job after graduating from Dr. Martin Luther College (Class of 1929). Adelheid evidently had learned that the call was coming and discussed it with her parents. Pastor Köhler’s letter mentions that Viola Oetchen, already a teacher at St. Lucas, was eager to have Adelheid as a roommate. For whatever reason, Adelheid made other living arrangements, renting a room from Albert and Margaret Nickel, two members of the church. The Nickels had a son named Hilbert, who sang in the St. Lucas choir ...

title:
Alice Ingram Hooker  
November 8, 1933 – July 23, 2019
content:
Alice Ingram Hooker, who spent her life serving a spectrum of Middle Tennessee with leadership and compassion, died Tuesday, July 23, 2019 at her Hunting Hollow home in Williamson County after an extended illness. Mrs. Hooker, a lifelong Nashvillian, will always be remembered for her forthright approach in making a difference, for her lasting kindness and for her deep desire to make Nashville a better place.

{{image:535}}

An advocate, devoted mother and friend, Mrs. Hooker was born in Nashville on November 8th, 1933 to the late civic leader Hortense Bigelow Ingram and Orrin Henry Ingram, a renowned business leader. She was preceded in death by her husband of 62 years, Henry Williamson Hooker; and by her brothers Erskine Bronson Ingram II and Frederic (Fritz) Bigelow Ingram.

Mrs. Hooker is survived by her sister Patricia Ingram Hart (Rodes Hart); and sister-in-law Martha Rivers Ingram; by her children Bradford Williamson Hooker (Jamie Ball) of Gloucestershire, England; Lisa Hooker Campbell (John Palmer Campbell III) of Nashville; and Timothy Ingram Hooker of Placida, Florida. She is also survived by grandchildren Henry Williamson Hooker II, Alexander Guy Hooker, Alice Hooker Campbell, Eileen Campbell Hart (Christopher Forbes Hart), John Palmer Campbell IV, Charles Ingram Hooker, Heather Louise Hooker and great-grand-daughter Evelyn Elisabeth Hart. She is also survived by many beloved nieces and nephews.

Mrs. Hooker attended Parmer School and Ward-Belmont, graduating from St. Timothy’s School in Baltimore Maryland. She earned a bachelor’s degree in American Culture at Vassar College. In 1956, she married childhood friend Henry Hooker. Mr. Hooker proposed to her while he was attending law school at Tulane University. After earning his degree, they returned to Nashville to raise their three children. They purchased land over 60 years ago and developed a beautiful farm – Hunting Hollow – in Williamson County. Quickly, they became a prominent young couple in Nashville’s equestrian, volunteer, business and society worlds.

Mrs. Hooker was a force. A consummate doer, she gave generously of her resources, time and wisdom. She was a lifelong community servant to almost every facet of our local non-profit sector. Her interests and contributions have ranged from education and children’s healthcare to historical preservation and cultural promotion. Her standards were high, her will and commitment unflinching. Mrs. Hooker was unafraid to roll up her sleeves and stand toe-to-toe – albeit graciously – with the male power brokers of her time. Consequently, she leaves an indelible imprint on Nashville’s community and culture. She was a Founder of the Nashville Memorial Hospital and then The Memorial Foundation, the Tocqueville Society Alpha Chapter of the United Way of Middle Tennessee, and The Friends of The Vanderbilt Children’s Hospital.

One of her most concerted efforts merged her passion for children’s welfare with her love of horses. In 2015, she and her husband were inducted into the Tennessee Sports Hall of Fame for that effort. They helped elevate the Iroquois Steeplechase from a unique sporting event into a spectacular citywide fundraiser for what is now the Monroe Carell Jr. Children’s Hospital at Vanderbilt. The Hookers were credited with initiating the fundraising union between the Volunteer State Horsemen’s Association and its Iroquois Steeplechase and the Friends of the Children’s Hospital – a 38-year alliance that has raised more than $10 million for the hospital. In 1986, 1994 and again in 2009, the Steeplechase was dedicated to the Hookers in appreciation.

The list of organizations that have also benefitted from Mrs. Hooker’s clear vision, tireless effort, and sound judgment includes: Vanderbilt University Medical Center Board, the Memorial Foundation, Canby Robinson Society, Iroquois Steeplechase, Cheekwood, the Nashville Symphony, United Way, Leadership Nashville, Project Pencil, the Garden Club of Nashville, the National Society of Colonial Dames of America in the State of Tennessee, Travellers Rest, the Middle Tennessee Pony Club, the Junior League of Nashville, St. Timothy’s School (Baltimore, MD) and the Ensworth School.

Her contributions of selflessness and service were gratefully acknowledged by her peers and serve as an example to those who follow. She received honors from many of these institutions for her volunteer leadership including Cheekwood, the Junior League of Nashville, Travellers Rest, the Memorial Foundation, United Way, the National Society of Colonial Dames of America, St. Timothy’s School and the National Multiple Sclerosis Society.

An avid horsewoman her entire life, Mrs. Hooker was a member of the Hillsboro Hounds foxhunting organization for 70 years, and, in 2019 became an Honorary Master. Mrs. Hooker also chaired the Iroquois Steeplechase Advisory Committee. She spent many years horse showing on the national circuit and served as a District Commissioner for Middle Tennessee Pony Club.

Passionate about gardening and conservation, she was a president of the Garden Club of Nashville and received the Land Conservation award for Williamson County. She also held many leadership positions at Cheekwood, including Board Chairman, Capital Campaign Director and Executive Committee member of the Swan Ball.

Mrs. Hooker – known as Nana to her grandchildren - was keenly interested in children’s health and education. She was devoted to Children’s Hospital for over 50 years, where she was a longtime board member. She became the first woman to serve as chairman of the Children’s Hospital Board and she was the first woman to chair the Vanderbilt University Medical Center’s Board, where she served for many years. She was also a permanent Trustee of Cheekwood, St. Timothy’s School, and The Ensworth School.

At the root of her life were her family and many friends. She delighted in hosting holiday events at Hunting Hollow, dinner gatherings in Wellington, Florida and Hunt Teas with her family in Cornersville, Tennessee.

Funeral services will be held at the First Presbyterian Church of Nashville on Tuesday, July 30th at 10 a.m. with burial immediately following the service at Mount Olivet Cemetery.

title: II. Girlhood in Ohio and Massachusetts
summary:
Alida’s prologue:  
*For more than a year, Emma has been coaxing and insisting that I write the memoirs of my family and about my early life. It seems to me a foolish undertaking, as every scrap of paper or records that might help went up in smoke long ago and there is only a very poor memory to depend upon.*
content:
We settled at once in a house on the corner of Bliss and Water streets, a double house, owned by a family named Kendall, who occupied the other half. My father was engaged in the feed business. His first care for me was to send me to school, and it pleased him when I was admitted to a grade above my years. This gave me a real heartache, for the girls said, “Oh it’s because you come from the country and are a country girl.” However, when they found out I could keep up with the class, their respect for me grew and we became good friends. How grand that large two-story brick building seemed to me after the little red schoolhouse. The teacher was Parmenus Strong, an unusually tall man, and his assistant an unusually small woman. I liked them both and apparently they liked each other, for they afterwards married. Mr. Strong was a good teacher and a good disciplinarian and was a stickler for promptness. I always had a horror of being late to school and remember one morning arriving breathless at the school door only to have it closed in my face. I turned and rushed home and could not be persuaded to return until the next day, when I came with a written excuse for absence — it being preferable to tardiness.

After three years, I with others entered the high school and there commenced for me four happy years. I made many, many friends among both girls and boys. We had good times in and out of school. Mr. Ariel Parish, the principal, was a fine man, a scholar, and a gentleman. He insisted upon good order and good manners. He was absolutely just. As an instance, I relate one experience. Up to this time, I had been used to oral spelling. On the first written exercise in the high school I missed twelve words out of fifty. Failing to cross a *t* or dot an *i* or change any other letter was accounted an error, but after one year of perfect work, we were excused from spelling. The words were to be written on narrow strips of paper with twenty-five words on each side. (We were on the last exercise of the year.) All the words began with an *E*, and to save time, because they were given out so rapidly, I wrote the capital *E* all down the lines. When the word “ennoble” was given out, I wrote it with a small *e* after the capital. That was an error. I went to Mr. Parish with tears but he in the kindest way made me see that the rule was broken and he could not in justice excuse me. I was required to spell six months more. Well, it didn’t hurt me a bit.

Up to that time, no class had ever graduated from this high school, so Mr Parish decided to graduate a class in the year 1856. The quarterly examination papers were to be used as a basis. The class was formed and a line of study mapped out. One after another of the members dropped out of our class until only eight remained — seven girls and one boy. We took the first diplomas ever presented by Springfield High School. We had the usual exercises in the old historic church before a house full of friends. Strange to say, Alida Lyman was the valedictorian.

I could not understand why. Our one boy, George Blanchard, seemed to me better qualified. The girls were Melia Newhall, Sarah Badger, Sarah Bates, Clarissa Stillman, Georgiana Moore, Juliet Smith, and Alida Lyman. Only three are now living: Sarah Bates, Clarissa Stillman and myself. I might mention here that the late Mr. Daniel R. Noyes of St. Paul attended this high school at the same time but never showed much interest in other pupils. Much as I enjoyed my classmates, my most intimate friends were not of the class. My first was Melissa Gerald. She was a tall slender girl with dark eyes and dark hair that hung in ringlets to her shoulders. She was of southern birth, an orphan living with her grandmother. She was romantic, intense, and full of enthusiasm, quite the opposite of me, but I loved her dearly. She loved to write letters, and nearly every day a long, finely written note was laid upon my desk. I really think any facility I may have in writing dates from that time, for I loved the notes and the sender, and they had to be answered in kind. I did my best. My first acute sorrow came when after one short week’s illness I saw my friend laid to rest.

There were other warm friends, but my last and best and dearest was Hattie Russell. (That is where Fred gets his middle name.) She lived nearby and many pleasant hours we spent together. She played the guitar and had a sweet voice. John Wilcox, whom she afterward married, and Edward Lathrop, her cousin, often joined us and made up a quartet. Julia Bemis was an other warm friend, also Warner Sturtevant, who later became her husband.There was Ben Wright, who lived on our street and who often carried my books to and from school, and Julius Hartwall, who insisted that I ride on his sled every time, and many others. How they came floating back in my memory. Many as from another world.

We attended the congregational church where Dr. Osgood was just rounding out his half century. The church matched the preacher in age. lt had a high gallery on three sides, a pulpit on a level with the gallery, and stairs winding up to it. The minister went in and shut the door; a sounding board was above his head. The pews were square boxes enclosed with doors. The choir sat in the gallery opposite the pulpit, and when they sang the congregation stood up and turned around facing them. When Dr. Osgood resigned, a younger man, Rev. Henry Parsons, took his place and showed more interest in the young people and children. I liked him very much.

I must tell you how I heard Jenny Lind, who sang in the same church. My father of course thought five dollars a preposterous sum to give to hear anyone sing, and besides this, he could not afford it. It was summertime and those church windows had to be open. With several others, I stood under a window and quite plainly heard her birdlike voice trill and soar as I had never heard anyone sing before.

My sister Lizzie was born in Springfield, Mass., in the year 1851, and there was great rejoicing over a girl. I was twelve years her senior and as she grew, I mothered and petted her. I curled her hair, dressed her dolls, and taught her to read. One night after Mother had given a party, a burglar entered our house. He stole the silverware, also ransacked all the drawers, took some pretty laces and a lovely bead bag which contained souvenir gold pieces. He also took father’s best coat and the money in his pockets and, worst of all, Mother’s false teeth, which had been left in a cup of water for the night. These were stolen for the gold plate, and poor Mother, who had suffered for a whole year for those teeth, had now to wait again several weeks for another set. The burglars also took cake and pies, and the empty plates were found on a neighbor’s steps.

Springfield was in those days a lively town, most beautifully shaded, with handsome residences and well-kept grounds. The arsenal on the hill was an interesting place to visit and we were only two blocks from the river, where boats were constantly passing. But all too soon good-byes had to be said to the place and to my dear friends, for my father made another move to Troy, N.Y., where with a Mr. Brown he manufactured and sold plows.

When I left Springfield it seemed to me that never again could I have as dear friends. I received about twenty ambrotypes and various small souvenirs. There were many tears at the train in parting.

Next: [Enter dashing young Charlie Bigelow](https://www.frauerica.org/Text/AlidaBigelow3.php)

title: III. Charlie Bigelow: Fine-looking, immaculately dressed
summary:
Alida’s prologue:  
*For more than a year, Emma has been coaxing and insisting that I write the memoirs of my family and about my early life. It seems to me a foolish undertaking, as every scrap of paper or records that might help went up in smoke long ago and there is only a very poor memory to depend upon.*
content:
Our final home in Troy was at 18 Grand Division Street. As usual, father’s first business was to put his boys in school. For a year I attended Mrs. Willard’s seminary as a day schooler. I didn’t enjoy it much nor make friends. It was a boarding school and I only saw the pupils during recitations. The church people were kind and soon began to make social calls. The church sewing societies were held in the afternoons with a supper at night to which the men were invited and to which they came — old men and young men — making it a real social party. I went with my mother and met many of the nicest young people.

Mrs. Waters was one of the prominent officers and came to call at our house. She asked if I had become acquainted and said that a very nice young man had made his home with her and that she would bring him to call. She soon did. He came, so fine-looking and dressed in an immaculate white suit. What girl could help almost losing her heart at first sight? (This caller was your father.) There was really a most charming set of young people in Troy. We had reading circles and sleigh rides in the winter and boat excursions on the river in the summer.

Dr. J.T. Duryea had recently been called as pastor of the church. He was just out of the seminary, a brilliant, enthusiastic and spiritual preacher. He brought his young bride there and she was as one of the girls. He gathered the young people in special meetings and many of us that winter chose “the better way” and united with the church. We lived in Troy only two and a half years. My father was in poor health and very much depressed. He felt that he had not long to live and wished to place his family where they could be looked after by relatives. He accordingly moved again to Madison, Ohio, and for twenty-five years afterward hardly had a sick day.

During these years my acquaintances with Charlie Bigelow had progressed, and one week before I left Troy (in October 1858) we became engaged. The following June, Charlie came out to Madison for a delightful week and we arranged to be married in October. We had a wedding breakfast for about fifty friends and were married immediately after by the Rev. Dr. Torrey. We stood up alone dressed for our journey. The bride in a checked brown and white silk, brown raglan and brown chip hat. I think there were ribbon strings tied under her chin. I think there were only three wedding gifts. Such things were not common then. Our wedding journey simply took us to our house at 18 Grand Division\* at Troy, N.Y., where our long “honeymoon” commenced. Charlie had purchased the house from father and remodeled it almost beyond recognition. With Mrs. Waters’ help, he put it in perfect order. As we came to the house, we found it all lighted up, and a little Irish maid opened the door. I wish I could put into words my impressions received at that open door. The soft lights, the furniture in perfect taste, the lovely carpets, the piano, the pictures, the long mirror between the windows, the writing desk, the lace curtains, and last of all the dining room, panels in green and oak, a table spread for two, and a dainty supper waiting. I had known of the purchase of the house, but the extensive alterations — of real elegance for the times — were to me a complete surprise.

That winter our house was a sort of center for the boys and girls to meet (we were only 20 and 24). Dr. and Mrs. Duryea ran in frequently and at the first communion season, Charlie united with the church. Our first party was on Thanksgiving Day. Mrs. Church and Mrs. Waters were cousins of Mother Bigelow (Charlie’s stepmother) and Charlie wished to return their many favors, so we invited their entire families — nine in all — to dinner. The turkeys, mince pies, etc., were good. Just before we sat down, my neighbor brought over two lovely dishes of chicken salad to add to the elegance of the repast. I had never seen or tasted chicken salad and did not know how to serve it, so we allowed it to stand untasted. Oh, those friends were kind to the green young housekeepers!

Anson and Emma were married in December 1859 and visited us in January 1860. We gave a party for them and later all of us went to visit Father and Mother Bigelow in Easton, N.Y. There I first realized the full force of the “Bigelow laugh” as they and Uncle Horace told stories and jokes about each other.

On October 27, 1860, our daughter Emma was born. Those were the days of the Civil War, and the interest everywhere was intense. We drove down to Albany one evening before the election of Lincoln to see a torchlight procession which was three miles long. Charlie’s enthusiasm for and loyalty to Lincoln were unbounded, but the Battle of Bull Run and the various defeats of the Northern Army in those early days of the war were very depressing. My brother Charles had enlisted in the army and my anxiety was great. Your father came down with diphtheria in the winter of 1861. This laid the foundation of his lifelong throat trouble, and his delicate health dated from that year.

On the 10th day of May, 1862, about 12 o’clock noon, there was an alarm of fire. As it came from the direction of Charlie’s place of business, he walked out to see if that was threatened. He soon returned having found that the fire had started in the roof of the old wooden railroad bridge which crossed the Hudson River. The wind was high, but blowing away from his place of business (the collar factory) and it was not in danger. Charlie was just recovering from the measles. He had been very ill, was quite weak, and when he came back he lay down upon the couch. Evidence of the big fire began to multiply. Large cinders and sparks floated through the air. We ran with pails of water to put them out as they came down on the window sills. The houses on the opposite side of the street caught on fire. Mrs. Bell brought over her little girl Fannie, four years old, and requested that we care for the child while she guided her old demented father to a place of safety so far away that he could not come back. Evidently she could not return either.

Soon a man rushed into our house crying, “Friends, you are surrounded by fire. Come away quickly or you never can.” Our table was set for dinner. Charlie gathered the silverware from it, his precious box of relics, the big Bible, etc., into a basket, while I ran for my precious baby Emma lying in her cradle. She was beautifully broken out with the measles. Mrs. Goodwin, a seamstress, had been preparing a complete outfit of summer clothing for the baby. She was still with me. The baby was in a night dress and a little long double gown. We snatched her from her cradle, tied a woolen hood and double brown veil over her head, and wrapped her in the most convenient thing at hand, which was a comfortable. Charlie sent the men with the basket hurriedly ahead with Mrs. Goodwin and the baby. My sister Lizzie had been spending the winter with me attending school.

Charlie took Fannie Bell under his arm and we walked out of our pretty home. How plainly I can see it now. No spark of fire on the inside of the house, but the sheds were burning in the rear and the front door was a charred surface. Walking through the narrow streets was not an easy matter. Every imaginable thing that people had dropped in their haste to escape was burning on the sidewalks. Big loads of furniture, from which the horses had been unhitched, were left to burn in the street. At the second corner below us some friends begged us to come in saying, “There is no danger here.” As Charlie was so weak and the child so heavy, we went in. They brought Charlie a glass of wine and made him rest on a couch. I took my stand by the window and before long noticed that the people who had been passing with their bundles were soon running back in the other direction. I went to Charlie and told him we were being surrounded again by the fire. He thought so too and again we set out only to meet fiercer flames than before. Our way led us toward the Union Depot and just before we reached it, the hot fires from a liquor saloon broke out upon us. Lizzie bore for a long time the scars from the boards that fell upon her. Charlie put his hand over little Fannie’s face to protect it but her little legs, hanging out behind were blistered through her stockings. His hand that covered her face was also burned. I threw a woolen shawl over my head and escaped being burned. The big depot was not then on fire but was destroyed later. It was nearly filled with beds and furniture that had been carried there. We climbed over the piles and caught our breath. Then we met a man who was a friend of Mrs. Bell and he took charge of little Fannie. In passing, I will say that he could not return the child to her mother until night, after the fire was out.

Fannie’s mother had fled north, but we went south. She was fairly frantic, not knowing the fate of her child. Charlie had told that man to take our baby to Mrs. Waters. When we reached the depot, we found the Waters’ house had burned. Fortunately, Charlie had taken the man’s address and we hurried on in that direction. A few blocks down, some friends of ours were on their front steps watching the fire and invited us in. Charlie was again much in need of rest, so he accepted the invitation. I went on to find my baby. The day was Saturday. I had the thinnest of slippers on my feet and wore a calico morning dress. I had to walk a mile, but I finally found the place, and the baby was crying for water. I will again say in passing that the two little figures in that house caught the measles from Emma and one died. This was the greatest tragedy of all. Mrs. Goodwin and I wrapped the baby up again in the unwieldy comfortable and carried her in turn, a block at a time, to the house where Charlie and Lizzie had stopped. During my absence, Dr. Searle had in some way found and prescribed for Charlie. He then ordered a carriage for us. We were glad to enter it with all of our worldly goods and drive six miles to Albany. Lucy, Charlie’s sister, had just heard that a big fire was raging in Troy and was wondering if it affected us. Thirteen lives were lost in the Troy fire and three hundred houses were destroyed. Our church and the homes of most of our friends were burned, and when we returned seeking our home we found only a heap of ashes, the location recognizable by the stone steps. Every keepsake and souvenirs of my girlhood days and our earliest married life was lost.

That was not the day of ready-made clothing, and with a sigh for the pretty clothes just finished for my baby, I bought gingham, a bolt of muslin and cambric. I rented a machine and with the help of Lucy and Mrs. Goodwin, made in two weeks a presentable wardrobe for the family. I procured an outfit for Lizzie from cousin Anne Jenkins of Albany, N.Y. We then left for Madison, Ohio, and later, leaving Emma with Mother, we went to Racine, Wisconsin, where Charlie and Anson arranged to go into the lumber business in Chicago. We then returned to Troy and boarded a few weeks in Lansingburg while Charlie disposed of his collar business to George O. Cluett and Brothers. On our return to Chicago, as we had no furniture, we boarded in the private family of Dr. Hitchcock on Prairie Avenue until August, when I went again to Madison, Ohio, where George was born on October 18, 1863. Chicago did not agree with Charlie. He had a carbuncle and boils which weakened him, and after George’s birth we went to Troy, N.Y. We consulted Dr. Searle, who advised him to go to St. Paul Minnesota.

St. Paul was a health resort in those days for persons with bronchial or lung trouble.

It was in the month of January 1864 and George was only thirteen weeks old, but we started at noon on January 4 for Chicago. We spent a couple of days there and left on January 7 for Minnesota Junction. There we remained four days. The weather was beautiful, clear and cold. Leaving Minnesota Junction, we traveled to La Crosse, Wisconsin, by train and from there by stage to Winona, Minnesota. Resting a day, we took another stage trip of sixty miles to Lake City and then the last long day also by stage from 8 a.m. to 7 p.m. to St Paul. It was zero weather every day, but the stage on runners was enclosed in canvas. The horses were changed every fifteen miles and while the change was being made, we could get out and stretch our tired limbs and make baby comfortable. Emma also had a chance to run and work off some of her restlessness.

It was a dark, cold winter night when we entered the International Hotel in St. Paul. (This hotel on the northeast corner of Jackson and Fourth Streets was later destroyed by fire.) Here we met Mr. and Mrs. E.M. Deane, who were ever after our dearest friends. St. Paul in 1864 was a city of about 8,000 inhabitants, all young people. (We never saw a grey head.) People came here either for their own health or that of some member of their family. There were only ten miles of railroad in the state from St.A health resort Paul to St. Anthony (now Minneapolis). Our mails came by stage in the winter and by steamboat in the summer. The Press kept us well posted on all the absorbing events of the war, which was not drawing to a close. My brother Wright had also enlisted in the army for a hundred days. This increased our anxiety, but both of my brothers came home unhurt.

It was almost impossible to rent a house in St. Paul, but after a week at the hotel, we secured a furnished house on Port Street (now 87th Street). It was cold and uncomfortable. We had proposed only to spend the winter in St. Paul, but Charlie steadily improved in health in the bracing air of Minnesota, so in the spring we bought a house on Dayton Avenue where we lived five years. There Charlie Junior was born.

In the fall of 1870 we built and moved into our home on College Avenue and there first had the luxury of a furnace and a bathroom with running water. Fred and Bert were born there. The house was burned in 1875, but rebuilt at once, the family meanwhile finding shelter in a little house on Third Street below College Avenue. In our new house, Deane was born, our bright, beautiful boy given to us for only nine and a half years.

Next to us lived our dear friends Mr. and Mrs. E.M. Deane. We were very intimate. All of us can remember their wonderful and never failing kindness and helpfulness, and especially their patience with the pranks of our mischievous children. That spot was our happy home for forty years. There we raised our family, our daughter to womanhood and our sons to manhood, and from there they all left us to form homes of their own. We loved the house with all its memories both gay and sad and hesitated long before leaving it. But in the year 1909, the location having grown undesirable, we built our present home at 485 Portland Avenue and moved into it on October 30th after celebrating on a beautiful golden day, October 5, 1909, our golden wedding at our Manitou Island cottage, White Bear Lake.

This climate which had given your father life and better health at first, in later years became too rigorous. The cold, dry air of winter affected his nerves and from the year 1883 we spent a part of each winter in a milder latitude. Charlie was fond of travel, loved lakes and mountains and all that was beautiful in nature. One winter we spent traveling in Europe with a company of St. Paul friends: B. Dean, his daughters Seila and Alice, Mrs. W. MacLaren and Jeanie, Bessie Taylor and Alice Bigelow. We spent several winters in California, another in Spain, enjoying its cities — Madrid, Seville, Cordova, Grenada, the Alhambra — finally sailing home from Gibralter. One winter we were in Nassau. One winter we hired a sailboat and sailed from Palm Beach, Florida, to Key West. The next year the tropical fruits, flowers, and ferns of Jamaica delighted us. We returned home by way of Cuba, Havana, and Key West and finished our trip in a sailboat to Tampa. One year we were eight weeks in Honolulu when George and Helen were living there. Two winters we spent in Tarpon Springs, Florida, and then for five consecutive years we were in Belle Air, Florida, where we made many dear friends and felt much at home. If you care for further details of these trips, you are referred to my diaries which were quite faithfully kept until someone presented me with a “Line a Day.”

Charlie lived to enjoy our new home scarcely two years and left us for his heavenly home on July 31, 1911. He was the dearest of fathers and the most devoted of husbands. How we miss his loving care. I esteem it my chiefest blessing that in spite of delicate health his life was spared so long.

This is a partial record of a long life, only those incidents that stand out boldly have been mentioned, the deep wells of joy and sorrow have not been reached. It has been a life full of blessings and now in its sunset I have you, Emma dear, who wished for the record, and my loving devoted sons and daughters and grandchildren to smooth my pathway to the end.

Postscript: [Alida’s letter about Charlie](https://www.frauerica.org/Text/AlidaBigelow4.php)

\*The typed transcript has the address as “48 Grand Division,” possibly a typographical error which conflicts with the first paragraph’s “18 Grand Division Street.” The first paragraph’s address is fuller and appears here for consistency.

title: IV. Alida’s letter about Charlie
summary: Alida Bigelow’s daughter Emma was insistent and encouraging about having a written memoir from her mother. Alida sent Emma this letter describing Charlie, his early days, and their years together. The file copy is undated however, so it is unclear whether this letter was a first attempt at memoir or a postscript to the finished work.
content:
Dear Emma:

Your father was a man beloved by all who knew him intimately. Many beautiful tributes to his worth and character have been written in letters and memorials by his friends in business and church. It is our delight to preserve them, but perhaps a few details that I can recall of his early family life may interest you.

{{image:3005}}

As you know, his father was Anson Bigelow, who lived in Easton, N.Y. Charlie was the youngest of six children. His oldest brother Erastus died of lung trouble when only twenty-six years old. His sister Mary died of the same trouble as the age of twenty-seven, leaving two children, Mary and Herbert Lourie.

His mother died of inflammation of the bowels (probably appendicitis) when your father was nine years old. His father was married again in about a year to Lydia M. Norton, a relative of his first wife, and Papa remembered very little of his own mother.

The children attended the country school near their home in Easton for the rudiments and later the academies in Union Village and Cambridge. As the boys grew up, life on the farm became more and more distasteful to them. Howard was the first to break away. He prepared for and entered Williams College, intending to become a physician. But after graduating and finding his health impaired, he took a trip west as far as Sioux City, Iowa. He was much impressed with the possibilities of the West but returned to the East and taught school for a short time. Mary Hayes was one of his pupils. He afterward married her and they went to live in Sioux City.

In the meantime, Anson had grown entirely discontented with farm life. Howard’s rosy accounts of the opportunities in the West appealed to him strongly. Mother Bigelow had a brother, sisters, and some cousins in Racine, Wisconsin. For that reason, Anson went to Racine and soon obtained a position in the lumberyards of Mr. Pendleton. Later the firm became Pendleton and Bigelow. Anson married Emma Ollmann and settled there.

Soon after Anson left home, Father Bigelow’s hopes of having a son succeed him on the farm were dashed to the ground, for Charlie, then only seventeen years of age, also begged permission to leave. He had often visited Troy, N.Y., with his father and loads of produce and knew something of that city. Leaving the old home on St. Patrick’s Day 1852, he first found employment with Gunnison and Stewart dry goods merchants and afterward with Billy Earle, a collar manufacturer who was later of the firm Earle & Wilson. He was in Mr. Earle’s employ three or four years when Mr. Maullin, a collar manufacturer in declining health, was looking for a partner and sought out Charlie Bigelow. Charlie’s father promised to furnish two thousand dollars as capital if needed, and Charlie became a partner under the firm name of Maullin and Bigelow. He was then just twenty-one years of age. Father Bigelow was never called upon for this money and the business flourished from the first.

During these years, Charlie made his home with Mrs. Waters, a niece of his stepmother, and was a welcome guest at Mrs. Church’s, another niece. He often visited his sister Lucy Griffin, then living in Albany, and whenever possible made a little trip to his “old home,” as he called it in Easton. With keen delight, he walked through the meadows, climbed the hill to Uncle Horace’s place, and partook of his store of chestnuts, apples, honey, and best of all had hearty laughs over bygone frolics.

I have mentioned the early deaths of Charlie’s oldest brother and sister. After he had an attack of diphtheria and the measles, Charlie’s throat and bronchial tubes seemed to him a menace of the same trouble. He therefore felt that it would be advisable to try a change of climate. The very morning of the Troy fire, he was expecting a party to look at our home with a view of buying it. The terrible fire settled that matter very quickly and disposed of our house. Soon after the fire, we went on a visit to Racine, Wisconsin. Anson Bigelow proposed that together they open a lumberyard in Chicago. Charlie agreed and Anson went to Chicago, found a suitable location for a yard, and commenced business. We went back to Troy, N.Y. Charlie sold his interest in the collar business to George B. Cluett, who was then a clerk, and invested this money in Bigelow Bros. of Chicago. He always retained his interest in that firm. After we came to St. Paul, his part in the management of the business was taken up by his brother Howard, who moved from Sioux City for that purpose.

For a year after our arrival in St. Paul in January 1864, Charlie did not engage in any business. He purchased a horse and buggy and with his wife and children took long drives into the country. In his diaries of that time there are many references to his delicate health, sore throat and frequent attacks of tonsilitis. Ever cheerful and hopeful, he was never an idle man. He cared for a garden and added many conveniences to our house on Dayton Avenue. He built a washing machine and worked a pair of slippers with a spread eagle on the toe, emblematic of war times. He was always helpful, sometimes even running a sewing machine for his wife.

Through the First National Bank of St. Paul, he made the acquaintance of Egbert and Horace Thompson, J.G and Henry Burbank, E.F. Drake, Amherst H. Wilder, John L. Merriam, and other influential men. He became interested in the railroad they were building to Sioux City, and was one of its directors. A town on that road was named Bigelow in his honor.

Early in 1865 Charlie and Mr. E.M. Deane and Mr. Murdock bought out the agricultural warehouse of a Mr. Jones on Third Street, also the agricultural implement department of Nicols and Dean and the plow department of Cheritree and Farwell. This company was afterward merged into the St. Paul Harvester Works for the manufacture of harvesters. It was located on Seventh Street. This business occupied all of Charlie's time until January 23, 1871, when he was elected secretary of the St. Paul Fire and Marine Insurance Co. On the death of J.C. Burbank on June 29, 1876, he became president of the company, which position he retained to the time of his death on July 31, 1911.

title: Two golden anniversaries, one remarkable woman
summary: The printed program for the March 2023 concert of the Providence Singers carried a version of this congratulatory note about two golden anniversaries: women’s intercollegiate ice hockey and the incorporation of the Providence Singers. Alli played a significant early role role in both.
content:
{{image:3006}}

Women at Brown University have been playing ice hockey at least since 1964 against whatever competition they could find. Many fans point to February 17, 1973, when the Brown Pandas were in Maine to play Colby College, as the game that finally put women’s intercollegiate ice hockey on the map. (The U.S. Olympic gold medal was still 25 years away.)

Colby scored late in the third period to win the match 3-2, but it was a close, fierce game complete with some great performances. Brown forward Allison McMillan, a music major, pianist, and member of the Brown Chorus, scored both Brown goals.

Also in 1973, the Providence Singers, a small community choral group, incorporated as a permanent nonprofit musical organization after two introductory seasons. Choral art and women’s hockey began 50 years of growth in Providence.

Alli kept both music and hockey interests alive and growing. She earned a master's degree in exercise physiology, played hockey in community leagues, helped kids in a youth league, and coached a JV girls high school team. But she was also a choir director, organist, accompanist, and, beginning in 1990, a member of the Providence Singers.

As the Singers Board president, she led the Singers during a period of strong artistic development and organizational renewal. In 2002, when the Singers revised its board structure, adopted new bylaws, and established a full-time professional staff, she became the Singers first executive director, serving for 12 seasons and representing the Singers nationally for two three-year terms as a board member of Chorus America. It was a period of strong, rapid growth, including performances with the Rhode Island Philharmonic and other regional orchestras, and the relocation of the Singers office and rehearsals to the Philharmonic’s Carter Center. Alli became Executive Director Emerita in May 2014.

Congratulations, Alli, and thanks from all of your family and friends.

title: The Ansons
summary: The Frau Erica archive knows of five forebears who bore Anson as their first or middle name.. There may well be more, considering the size of families a couple generations in the past. Here are the five. If you know of others, please [contact the archivist](https://www.frauerica.org/Format/Contact.php?who=heintz).
content:
**Anson Bigelow** (December 20, 1794 – September 15, 1869)  
was the Bigelow-McMillan connection with the Pells, eventually including Claiborne deBorda Pell, the famous Rhode Island senator. Anson and his wife Elizabeth Moores had six children, including Charles Henry, Sr. (“C.H.”, Nana’s grandfather) and Anson Alexander, great-grandfather of the senator.

**Anson Alexander Bigelow** (November 7, 1833 – October 13, 1895)  
and his wife Emma Ullmann had two children who survived to adulthood: Nelson Pendleton and Emilie. (Their first child, Charles Henry, died before his first birthday.) Nelson, cousin to Charles Henry Bigelow, Jr. (Nana’s father), married Sophia Dallas deBorda. Their daughter Matilda married Herbert Claiborne Pell, Jr., and their only child was the famous Rhode Island U.S. Senator Claiborne deBorda Pell. Nelson and Charles Henry Bigelow Jr. being first-cousins, their kids — Matilda Bigelow and Molly Bigelow (Nana) were second-cousins. Nana and Sen. Pell were second-cousin once removed (a generation apart).

The archivist, husband of Mary Allison McMillan, ran a small press opportunity when Sen. Pell visited Brown University and had a chance to ask about the family ties. The Senator’s face brightened immediately. He knew the entire history. Matilda and Herbert Pell divorced in Paris — amicably, from all accounts — and Herbert married Mildred Olive Bigelow, Matilda’s cousin, who went by Olive. The Senator suggested that his father loved Bigelow women so much that he married two of them.

{{image:637}}

**Anson Alexander Bigelow** (January 1, 1897 – December 3, 1958),  
Matilda’s brother, a 1917 graduate of the U.S. Naval Academy, was born in Chicago to Nelson Bigelow and Sophia Dallas. He entered the Naval Academy at age 16, one of the youngest midshipmen ever accepted. His class graduated two months early to allow newly commissioned officers to enter service during World War I. He later served with distinction in the Pacific during World War II.

Anson married his first wife, Carlotta Havermeier, in 1925. They had a son, Theodore, and a daughter. On July 4, 1945, Anson married Josephine “Fifi” Widener of Philadelphia. Anson and Fifi moved to Palm Beach, Florida, in 1946. Horse-racing and -breeding enthusiasts, they spent their summers in Saratoga Springs, New York, for the racing season, and visited Hialeah in Florida frequently during the winter.

**Albert Anson Bigelow** (July 31, 1872 – February 17, 1919)  
would have been Nana’s uncle, a brother of her father Charles Henry, Jr. He used his middle name rather than Albert and appeared to use “A. Anson Bigelow” for official documents. He died tragically about five months before Nana was born, suffering a heart attack while swimming in the ocean off the Florida coast. He was 46.

**Anson Edouard Nickel** (October 24, 1987 – )  
-

title: Elizabeth Ann Mueller
summary: This obituary was published June 3, 2021, in the *Chicago Tribune*.
content:
{{image:597}}

**Elizabeth A. Mueller** – Daughter of William L. and Lillie Mueller (Daiss). Born November 7, 1934, in LaGrange, Ilinois, the youngest of eight Mueller children; pre-deceased by half-siblings Erica, Cordula, William, and siblings Lillie, Frieda, Caroline, and Carl. Beth attended St. John's Lutheran School in LaGrange through eighth grade and was a 1951 graduate of Lyons Township High School, received a BSE from Valparaiso University in 1956 and in 1963 received an MLS from the University of Chicago.

She began her professional working life as a teacher of grade school students in Hicksville, New York, and Mahwah, New Jersey, returning to the Chicago area to work at J. Walter Thompson Advertising as a researcher, where she worked on the Ford Mustang project among others. In 1963, she graduated from the University of Chicago, beginning a career with the Chicago Suburban Library System in Cook County, rising to become Consulting Services Director. Headquartered in Burr Ridge, Illinois, she worked with the 115 libraries in the Cook County system, helping many small municipalities through bond referenda, architect selection, grant applications, and public approvals to build or expand their own libraries. She received the Illinois Librarian of the Year Award in 1993.

Beth retired in 1989, moving to North Carolina where she became Director of the Appalachian Regional Library System including Watauga, Ashe, and Wilkes Counties. There she oversaw the expansion of the library system, adding a new Watauga County library in Boone and building a new library in in Wilkes in 1999.

Beth retired (again) from a formal role in 2000 remaining active in support of library, church and civic activities. She served as Program Officer of the High Country Branch of American Association of University Women North Carolina, and as Treasurer and Volunteer Coordinator of Watauga Literacy Association. She believed in the power of books and education and loved to drive, as her father had. After retiring, she spent time delivering books to rural families with children through the Reading and Rolling program.

A member of Grace Evangelical Lutheran Church in Boone since 1993, Beth was active in the Fellowship Ministry and the Finance Team as well as the Lizzie-Estelle Women’s Circle, and the Choir. She participated in an aid trip to Haiti.

Beth was a thorough professional, but also a warm and generous person. She was always interested in the people around her, as well as her extended family, attending family celebrations, often with her older sister Frieda, all across the United States, keeping in touch with dozens of relatives. She was the family archivist, collecting old photographs and memorabilia. A lover of music, she traveled widely in Europe with friends, attending the Mozart Festival in Salzburg Austria, and bringing her sister Kiki (Caroline) to the Passion Play in Oberammergau. Following a Mueller tradition of Sunday coffee cakes, she opened her home to many, sociably or when they were in need. She was always a sympathetic ear for her many nephews and nieces and their children. She owned several beloved dogs, including Emma and lastly Princess. She participated fully in the several communities in which she lived, as well as many national and international charitable organizations.

Beth is survived by 13 of her 19 nieces and nephews, and dozens of grand-nieces and -nephews. Beth made many very close friends over the years, keeping ties for 65 years or more, and she made new friends constantly; her several dear friends in Boone, North Carolina, kept her company and cared for her until the end.

A memorial service for Beth will be held at 2 p.m. Sunday, June 6, 2021 at Grace Evangelical Lutheran Church, 115 East King Street, Boone, NC 28607.

title: Carl de Haas and *Winke für Auswanderer*
summary: Carl de Haas, great- and great-great-grandfather to the McMillans of White Bear Lake, arrived on the Wisconsin frontier in 1847. *Winke für Auswanderer,* his booklet of advice for people considering emigration, was widely available in Germany and went through at least two printings and an expanded second edition.
content:
In May 1847, following what he called his “student years” at the university in Bonn (he received a Doctor of Philosophy degree in 1844), Carl de Haas emigrated to the Wisconsin frontier north of Fond du Lac. A year earlier, he had joined a group considering emigration to Texas but abandoned those plans because of an inhospitable climate and the political and social uncertainties of the War with Mexico.

Aggressive sales agents for ship operators, innkeepers, even for the slow horse-drawn canal boats of the American interior were common in Germany then. Reliable information about emigration and life in the New World, however, was difficult to find. Carl planned to fix that. For his own emigration, he relied on published letters from Johann Mentis and others, written from their homesteads in Wisconsin and elsewhere.

He made the trip from his family home in Elberfeld (now Wuppertal), traveling 20 miles west to Düsseldorf, up the Rhein by steamer to Rotterdam, by ship from Rotterdam to Cherbourg, and then by steamship to New York, a 16-day voyage. His traveling companions included a brother Richard, a nephew Eduard, and six others, including two children. They carried their belongings in 14 chests, large and small.

Carl kept careful notes and within a few months of the group’s arrival in Wisconsin had produced *Winke für Auswanderer,* a densely written 92-page booklet stuffed with advice on exchange rates, what to pack, how to farm, what to avoid, how to select a team of oxen, evaluating prices of goods and services, arranging transportation from New York to Wisconsin, avoiding agents, and much more. A photocopy of the original German booklet, together with an English translation, is part of the online collection at the [Wisconsin Historical Society](https://content.wisconsinhistory.org/digital/collection/tp/id/65507). The Frau Erica site includes a version of the *Winke für Auswanderer* translation organized in nine chapters, clickable at left.

**The de Haas family in the New World**

Seven and a half million German immigrants arrived in the United States between 1820 and 1870, settling predominantly in the upper midwestern states. (The U.S. census for 1840 counted slightly more than 17 million residents.) In 2019, according to the U.S. Census Bureau, Germans remained the largest self-identified U.S. immigrant group, with 43 million descendants.

By the time they settled on the 40-acre farm Carl had bought in Calumet, Wisconsin, the de Haas emigrants had found much that was familiar. The German language could be heard on the streets of cities and towns. Commerce could be conducted in several currencies — Prussian Thaler, *Silbergroschen,* schillings — and many stores and hotels supported transactions in foreign currencies by posting signs with rates of exchange. Much of the advice in *Winke für Auswanderer* involves equipment and purchases for farming and the current prices paid for livestock or bushels of grain. Most of Carl’s German readers were farm families drawn to the New World by fertile soil, affordable land, and far better prospects.

Farming, however, apparently lost its appeal for Carl, and he soon left the agricultural life. In 1848, a year after Carl had bought the original farm, the de Haas group bought rights to a 160-acre parcel on a small lake about four miles east of the farm. There they tried to establish a brewery — Carl’s nephew Eduard had trained as a brewer in Germany — but the brewery does not appear to have lasted. Richard bought the land in July 1850, sold it to Eduard in January 1851, and then returned to Germany. Eduard sold the land that March. Carl himself was already living in the village of Calumet where he was running an inn and writing about emigration for German newspapers in Milwaukee.

Already in Germany, Carl had an interest in writing. He had written articles for newspapers, produced some poetry, and one of his dramas had been staged in Elberfeld. Writing seemed a way forward. His *Winke für Auswanderer*, published in 1847, was doing well and was widely read in Germany. German-language publishing was thriving in the Midwest, particularly in Chicago and Milwaukee. *Die Abendschule, Germania* and many other newspapers and magazines had found a ready and dedicated audience. (Frau Erica herself was an *Abendschule* columnist.)

In 1850, Carl moved east to Buffalo, where he began a 25-year career as a writer and newspaper editor. He married Catherine Burg. Their first four children — Frederick, Carl, Edward, and Emma — were born in Buffalo. The family then moved to Detroit, where Richard (1863) and Bertha (1866) were born. Carl and his family returned to Fond du Lac in 1871, where Carl founded a successful German newspaper, the *Norwestlicher Courier.*

[Frederick](https://www.frauerica.org/Families/643.php), Carl and Catherine’s first child (born 28 August 1851), spent his adult life in St. Paul, Minnesota. He and his wife Johanna Hauser had six children — Paul, Virginia, Louis, Elsa, Louise, and Leona. [Elsa](https://www.frauerica.org/Families/627.php), born 30 November 1886, married Malcolm McMillan. They had two sons: Malcolm Jr. and [Richard](https://www.frauerica.org/Families/84.php).

title: *Winke für Auswanderer:* Hints for Emigrants
summary: Carl, his brother Richard, and nephew Eduard began their trip to New York by river steamer from Düsseldorf to Rotterdam, and by sea from Rotterdam to Havre and Cherbourg. It was an early introduction to seasickness, poor service, inedible food, overcharging, and too much baggage. Still, he encouraged others to make the trip.
content:
{{image:602}}

Upon my departure from Germany I promised friends and acquaintances that I would send them, within the year, a complete report of my observations during the journey from Elberfeld, Germany, to Calumet, Wisconsin, and particularly of my experiences here.

I have now been in Wisconsin a quarter of a year, and it is time to fulfill, to the best of my ability, that promise. It is, of course, rather presumptuous that I, a young farmer in the midst of activities that harmonize badly with writing, should take up the pen with a hand rough and stiff from the unaccustomed work. I would have preferred to let my observations have time to mature. But there is one thought that outweighs all considerations which might work against my promise: I hope to be useful to my friends with this report, to prevent some from overhasty steps, and perhaps to encourage many to come over and share our lot.

As I know from earlier experience, a letter from America is soon read to shreds, especially on the poor paper one gets here. I prefer therefore, to have these pages printed. Perhaps other countrymen beyond my circle of acquaintances will draw valuable advice from these hints.

— Dr. Carl de Haas  
 Calumet, October 23, 1847

In 1844, toward the end of my student years in Berlin, I and two young men who were my close friends decided to emigrate to America. Our goal was Texas; the so-called “Nobles’ Association” was to make the arrangements for our emigration. Everything had been considered and arrangements had been made, when we read some reliable reports and letters which convinced us that Texas, beautiful and rich as the country is, could not in its present state be a goal for the emigrant who seeks a peaceful, free life in nature, not merely adventure. The hostility of the numerous Indians there has to the present day prevented emigrants transported there from taking possession of the land sold to them, and even the cities which have been laid out are by no means safe from their robbing attacks. Furthermore, the climate is tropical, which is not favorable to the German constitution, and the higher spots where the climate is more bearable for us, are surrounded by unhealthy regions. Perhaps the Nobles’ Association will be in a better position, when the Mexican War is over, to fulfill their promises thus far so poorly kept. Then perhaps the new state of Texas will be safer and will be a really livable place for the emigrant who finds the German climate too cold.

To be brief, our plan fell through. I joined the faculty of my home town (Elberfeld) vocational and high school as temporary teacher of mathematics and chemistry, and the once serious plans to emigrate vanished from my thoughts.

But my hopes were not to be abandoned permanently. In the fall of last year several young men in Elberfeld worked together to get information on the best goal for German emigration. On the second Christmas day 1846 I met some of them and joined their group. From that time on my resolve was unshakable, although I had to keep my plans secret up to a few weeks of my departure because of my teaching position. A letter printed in the Barmen newspaper, written by an emigrant from Wesel to his family, first called our attention to Wisconsin and to the region around Milwaukee. Other letters from an emigrant, Johann Mentis of Friesdorf near Bonn, praised Fond du Lac county and the Calumet colony, to the north of Milwaukee, as being a place especially suited for German settlement.

Several members of our society tried to sell their possessions but found no buyers because of the extremely bad times in Germany and had to put off their departure. The time of departure was so delayed for others that in May only eight men, three women, and five children prepared to leave. And then even these divided. Two men with their wives and three children (Cabinet maker F. and the Stonemason K.) departed the beginning of May. They took cabin passages on the three-master Flora from Antwerp and were very satisfied with their 32-day trip. They could not praise highly enough the captain’s good treatment of them, as well as the behavior of the crew, and they judged as excellent their food and drink at the captain’s table.

With my brother Richard, engraver and modeler; my nephew Eduard, brewer; Büttner, agriculturist; Langefeld, baker (all unmarried), and the clerk Kohl and his wife and two children, I began the long journey on May 17. We had made reservations 14 days previously in Elberfeld through the agent of the well-known Dr. Strecker for the first of the three French steamers which were to make the voyage to New York twice a month from the middle of May on.

The afternoon of May 17 while I sat with my brothers and friends in a public resort outside of the town, the agent sent us tickets for the steamer leaving that same evening from Düsseldorf, reporting that he had just received them from Dr. Strecker. Anyone who has ever got ready for such an important trip will be able to imagine our terrible situation. It is a real miracle that we arrived at seven o’clock in the evening all packed at the Düsseldorf-Elberfeld station. Of course, the parting from parents and brothers and sisters was cut short. I saw my father barely long enough to say farewell. Some chests had still to be packed and marked, and Prussian money exchanged for French. It was very pleasant for us that the news of our departure had quickly made the rounds and that almost all of our friends could gather at the station. We drank farewell with one glass of May-punch — *Maitrank*, white wine flavored with woodruff or asperula odorata (perhaps the last for us). With the general cry of “Hurrah, America” and the waving of hats from the windows of the moving train, we lost sight of the homeland.
