We remain transfixed by the march of generations. Overlapping visits from the Brooklyn and Richmond families had all four grandchildren spending the Fourth of July at Oma and Opa’s house. Archie, 3, and Leia, not quite 7 months, arrived July 1 with Anson and Reva for a four-day visit. Thomas, almost 5, and James, almost 3, arrived on July 3 with Susan and George.

It wasn’t all Brios. We harvested a nice crop of sour cherries, filled the freezer, and made a dozen jars of jam. Pitting the cherries used to be a chore, but when you show little kids how to squish out the pits, it becomes a wonderful summer game with overtones of Tom Sawyer whitewashing the fence. And what gardener would not enjoy seeing a 3-year-old grandson excitedly yank beets out of the ground for the evening dinner?

Certainly, there was a garden this year, though the blueberry bushes put out more leaves than berries, which was disappointing. This growing season’s major discovery was succotash, a deep purple-maroon dry bean which, when combined with corn and a few options, yields succotash, the eponymous dish said to have been served at the first Thanksgiving. Mark has grown lots of heirloom vegetables, but succotash is an ancient heirloom original to Rhode Island’s native Narragansett tribe. They were among the indigenous New Englanders who schooled the Pilgrims in North American agriculture, including the trick of growing certain crops together, chiefly corn, succotash and squash. It’s a hardy, strong climbing vine that we’ll see much more of next year.

Change is coming to the manse. A second-floor master bedroom doesn’t make as much sense as it once did, so we are moving into guest quarters on the first floor, probably next week. The first-floor bedroom has a new bed, a new television, and a new HomePod mini, which provides an intercom service to other HomePod minis in our kitchen and in Mark’s second-floor office, in addition to all the other Siri-inspired services. We also have a full pallet of green-dipped shingles stored in the garage, a happy ending to what could have been a nightmare supply-chain problem that would have delayed completion of the project until sometime in 2023. They will be installed on the south exterior wall overlooking the garden, the final piece of a home improvement project begun in 2019.

We are slowly getting used to life without weekly choral singing. We both sang *Messiah* in 2019, which turned out to be the Singers’ last public performance until last month. Mark kept going with the virtual performance season last year, listening to the accompanist on his AirPod Pros and singing into his iPhone’s voice memo app. It was an interesting experience, and the resulting virtual choir performances were pretty good, but five or six dozen voices singing by themselves wasn’t the same. (Listen to a sample at <https://www.providencesingers.org/> ) We are now both emeritus/a members and try to stay in touch.

There is one touching bit of choral news, though. In March, the Providence Singers will offer the world premiere of a newly commissioned work by Julian Wachner for chorus and orchestra, part of the organization’s 50th anniversary celebration. It’s a setting of Sara Teasdale’s “A November Night.” Artistic Director Christine Noel dropped by the house and gave us copies of the choral score and the full score. We were delightfully stunned to discover that the piece is “Dedicated to Allison McMillan and Mark Nickel in grateful recognition of their commitment to choral music and their long history of leadership with the Providence Singers.”

The work’s title: *We Two Alone*.

With love and best wishes,

**Mark and Alli**  
[mark-nickel@cox.net](mailto:mark-nickel@cox.net) 401-835-1913  
[allison-mcmillan@cox.net](mailto:allison-mcmillan@cox.net) 401-225-3659

title: Christmas 2022
summary: Annual Christmas letters get a mixed reception: tossed unread, browsed lightly, responded to. In the aggregate, though, they have some historical value, some bits of information about who’s doing what. So here’s the archive.
content:
{{image:663}}

December 15, 2022  
The Third *Stollen* of Advent

Dear Friends,

We haven’t made it halfway through December and we’re already awash in joyful good news. Waiting for us at breakfast Wednesday (12/7) was email announcing that Olivia Eirene Crawley Kantikou (Kantikou Crawley in the American format), Dan and Aliki’s first child, had arrived that morning in Madrid, serene and beautiful. Olivia’s birth came five days after her cousin Adriana Sánchez Baquerizo arrived (12/2), the first child of our niece Elsa and Abel, also in Madrid. And three days later (12/10), their American cousin Leia celebrated her second birthday with friends and cupcakes on a Saturday morning in Brooklyn. Everyone is home, healthy, very happy, and resting a bit. (We are too.) It’s been a wonderful December.

Zoom and Facetime are no longer new technologies, but we are still amazed, not just at the news, photos and conversations from Spain, but at how easily and naturally digital technologies have worked their way into our evolving household routine. A little bedside gooseneck holds Alli’s iPhone for audio chats; a steadier floor stand puts her iPad at just the right spot for video Zooms and Facetime sessions. Our three HomePod minis answer questions, provide intercom service in the house or between house and car, consult encyclopedias when necessary, and, as grandson Archie demonstrated for us, will tell jokes on request (with considerate warnings before offering any truly wretched puns).

All of this — the technology and especially the calls and video sessions it supports — has helped Alli maintain a positive outlook in her third year of living with MSA. So have the ramps, which make the entire first floor accessible, connect to the deck through the kitchen sliding door, and guarantee all-weather access to the whole world by running from the laundry room through the garage and down to driveway level, where the Honda CR-V awaits.

And then there’s what we call the Studebaker, an all-terrain wheelchair (French technology) that’s low-slung, collapsible, recumbent, supportive, and customizable — a birthday gift from Alli’s siblings. We’ve joined the Norman Bird Sanctuary, explored its hiking trails, and met deer, turtles, Monarch butterflies, assorted birds, and the resident great blue heron. We’ve hiked dozens of times at the Sachuest Wildlife Refuge, both the 1.5-mile Ocean View Trail and the 2.5-mile version, which adds what we have dubbed the Mink Trail, after several encounters with the eponymous little weasel. We seem to cut quite a figure, often drawing compliments on the Studebaker (“Hey — Love the wheels!”) and even one on Mark’s pushing technique. One fellow hiker ran out to the parking lot as we were packing up, examined the chair, got the model number, vendor’s email address and phone number, and set off to order one for his wife. Exactly what they needed, he said.

It was a fairly big year out in the garden: a bumper crop of succotash, buckets of cherry tomatoes, a freezer full of blueberries, plenty of popcorn, enough basil for a dozen jars of pesto, lots of rutabagas (“Enough is too much,” as Popeye said), herbs in abundance. But the big news is our two little heirloom apple trees in the front yard. One is a Newtown Pippin, Thomas Jefferson’s favorite (which he missed while on a diplomatic trip to Europe: “They have no apples here to compare with our Newtown Pippin.”) The other is a Northern Spy, a New England favorite since about 1800. They arrived as mere twigs but had a healthy first year with good leaves and strong growth. We hope for fruit in three years. (I see that Trees of Antiquity has sold out of both for the 2023 season.)

*Stollen* number three is in its first rising, continuing a Christmas tradition that dates at least to 19th-century pre-emigration Germany. We’re now looking forward to holiday family visits from Brooklyn (Anson, Reva, Archie, Leia) and Richmond (Susan, Thomas, James) and our first face-to-face with Olivia later in the spring. Meanwhile, the household continues to evolve as age and MSA require — smart plugs running room lights here and there, railings on both sides of staircases. Visitors here will have met our mother-daughter team of certified nurse assistants, who have added a much appreciated Jamaican brightness to the daily routine, Monday through Friday, for nearly the entire year.

On the whole, we’re doing well, making adjustments as necessary, and exploring some new interests. Among them is the development of a fascinating small ecosystem just outside Alli’s window that centers on a squirrel-proof bird feeder provided by Dan and his cousin Molly. We observe a constant parade of little birds (finches, chickadees, sparrows), who yield to the larger cardinals, jays, and woodpeckers, who in turn clear out quickly when the large, flashy northern flicker cruises in for a meal. And down below, the hated squirrels, always a threat to gardens, make restitution by cleaning up any seeds and grains the birds may drop onto the deck. Even the wide-body mourning doves waddle around pecking and keeping the place tidy. (We are so *au courant.* This morning’s online NY Times reviews a new book, [*Slow Birding: The Art and Science of Enjoying the Birds in Your Own Backyard*](https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/14/realestate/after-a-frantic-year-its-time-for-slow-birding.html), by a Washington University biology professor and animal behaviorist. Life lists and competitive birding are so out of date; our style of extended observation is the new trend.)

We hope this finds you happy, healthy, creatively engaged, and able to take a step back during the holiday season for much needed rest and reflection.

With love,

**Alli and Mark**  
Reach us both at [mark-nickel@cox.net](mailto:mark-nickel@cox.net) 401-835-1913 (We are so *au courant.* This morning’s online NY Times reviews a new book, [*Slow Birding: The Art and Science of Enjoying the Birds in Your Own Backyard*](https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/14/realestate/after-a-frantic-year-its-time-for-slow-birding.html), by a Washington University biology professor and animal behaviorist. Life lists and competitive birding are so out of date; our style of extended observation is the new trend.)

title: Christmas 2023
summary: Annual Christmas letters get a mixed reception: tossed unread, browsed lightly, responded to. In the aggregate, though, they have some historical value, some bits of information about who’s doing what. So here’s the archive.
content:
{{image:736}}

December 10, 2023  
The Third *Stollen* of Advent

Dear Friends,

Early one very warm morning last summer – Thursday, trash day – I walked out to see whether the bins I’d set out the night before were empty and ready to roll back into the garage. That’s a homeowner’s weekly chore here, but this time I stopped short. There at the end of our driveway were two little brothers in their pajamas, sitting cross-legged on a small mat, munching breakfast bars and waiting patiently for the garbage truck. Trash collection was a compelling interest at the time, and Thomas and James, then 6 and 4, had hoped that their visit to Opa and Oma’s house might include a trip to the dump. Then they had a better idea.

Soon enough, a huge green and yellow Waste Management vehicle swung into view at the top of the hill and worked its way down Gideon Lawton Lane to the home at 101. These trucks are solo-staffed mechanical giants; the operator seemed to size up the situation immediately. “Hey guys!” he said, as he climbed down, pulled a lever, and started the mechanical arm toward the 96-gallon bin. Thomas and James stared wide-eyed as the arm yanked the bin up and over the truck, turned it upside-down, dumped its contents, and zoomed it back to the original streetside position. The arm shifted a bit, and the 96-gallon recycling bin followed suit. “That’s it, guys. You have a lot of fun today, okay?” the garbage man said, climbed into the cab, waved, and drove on. He had made their day; they almost certainly made his.

Almost six months later, that vignette remains vivid, and not just on Thursdays. It’s part of a powerful grandchild tapestry in progress for seven years now with many more adventures still to come. There have been hikes at Sachuest Wildlife Refuge with everyone. There’s Archie, 5, and his chess game, which now includes bishop sacrifice, discovered check, and other advanced strategies (he’s 2-0 in games with Opa). Leia, turning 3 today, loves to explore Oma’s Steinway, finding the low boomy notes at one end of the keyboard and tinkly high notes at the other (she seems to prefer the boomy ones), while at the other end of the house, Oma is cheered and profoundly delighted to hear such earnest, focused *klimpering*\*. And early this November, 11-month-old Olivia and her family visited from Madrid. She’s a strong, happy little girl whose eyes take in everything around her and whose ears are taking in a multilingual stream of English, Spanish, and Greek. She can pull herself up, stand, and make tentative foot movements that suggest walking may not be far off. Grandparenting is turning out to be a wondrous, life-altering, immensely rewarding experience. Grandkids have a way of refreshing perspectives.

Life here on the Lane continues to evolve – a bit slower, perhaps, to accommodate a higher median age but also a bit more varied. Although we can’t travel as we have in the past, there are now visits from Alli’s friends and classmates, including her doubles partner from a memorable high school tennis career, classmates from Brown, siblings and other relatives, plus Zoom and FaceTime sessions. Del and Shantel, Alli’s weekday caregivers, will soon be starting their third year with us and continue to provide highly skilled and dedicated support for Alli’s steady struggle with MSA. They feel like extended family.

A small literary project is just getting underway, thanks to the arrival this fall of the *Sängerbote* from brother Timothy’s house to my office via FedEx. The *Sängerbote* is a 700-page hardbound, five-year (1913-17) compilation of a German-language music and literature quarterly published in St. Louis. Several Mueller family forebears were among many dozens of contributors, including our maternal great-grandmother Adelheid Rickmeyer Mueller (a.k.a.., Frau Erica), two of her children (including our grandfather Ernst) and others both on the family tree and standing nearby. It’s not just a translation project, which goes very slowly. The *Sängerbote* was at the core of Mueller family life before dial telephones, television, social network, and the rest of what the 20th century would bring. It also provides a portal into German immigrant life in the WWI years, when Lutheran congregations switched to English services and German was spoken less often outside the home. The May 1916 *Sängerbote* even featured a three-verse *“Sterne und Streifen”* — "Stars and Stripes,” a singable German version of what would become the National Anthem.

The garden, always an important part of life on the Lane, had significant highs and lows this season. There’s newly rebuilt fencing on the south side, expanded culinary herb plantings, and successful installation of a user-designed, handcrafted, efficient soaker-hose watering system. We weren’t expecting any fruit from the heirloom apple trees in their second season, but the Newtown Pippin (Thomas Jefferson’s favorite apple) did manage to serve up a couple really delicious pieces. Likewise, our two young Damson plum trees at the other end of the property yielded enough fruit to cook down an inaugural batch of *Pflaumenmus* (plum jam). The Corn Palace produced a tasty bumper crop of heirloom white popcorn, we have lots of succotash, and the pantry is well-stocked with blueberry-rhubarb and other jams.

But there was sad garden news too. Our three dwarf Montmorency cherry trees, faithful annual source for sour cherry tarts and jars of jam for more than a decade, were overrun with black knot, a highly contagious airborne fungal disease that affects cherry and – horrors! – Damson plum trees. There is no reliable cure; staff-intensive hand pruning buys only a little time at significant expense. On the advice of an arborist whose own cherry trees had the same problem, the three will be cut down, carted away whole (chipping would fill the yard and neighborhood with contagious plant fungus) and burned. Their stumps will be ground out and the Damson plum trees will be sprayed with a fungicide as a precaution. We’re hoping for the best.

Someone once asked Martin Luther what he would do if he knew the world would end tomorrow. Brother Martin said he would plant a tree today. Hmmm. Sounds a bit like us. Maybe more apple trees?

We hope this season finds you and yours in good health, happy in spite of the mean-spirited Zeitgeist, up to your ears in creative projects, able to kick back a bit at year’s end, and looking confidently toward a much better year in 2024.

With much love,

**Alli and Mark**  
Reach us both at [mark-nickel@cox.net](mailto:mark-nickel@cox.net) 401-835-1913

title: Christmas 2024
summary: Annual Christmas letters get a mixed reception: tossed unread, browsed lightly, responded to. In the aggregate, though, they have some historical value, some bits of information about who’s doing what. So here’s the archive.
content:
{{image:3049}}

December 21, 2024  
The Second *Stollen* of Advent

Dear Friends,

From the outside looking in, life at Opa and Oma’s home on the Lane may seem a bit monochromatic this year, perhaps in need of a little pepping up, a bit more variety. True, we have no exotic Galapagos trips to report, no beachcombing, no travel this year, but we are creating a different, adaptive and necessary lifestyle.

Healthcare professionals use a Hoyer lift to move patients around, but we have adapted that technology. We are now skilled operators of the “Flying Oma Device.” With a little hydraulic pumping, Oma rises mystically from her bed in a stylish blue and green hammock. She flies a few feet to her left, hovers briefly over the wheelchair, and gradually descends. A trip up the ramp and down the hall leads to breakfast at the kitchen table. In summer, Alli then heads safely down a short ramp to the deck, where she takes the Flying Oma Device to the chaise longue. Grandkids have been amazed. If it’s rainy or not warm enough, Alli heads to Dan’s leather recliner in the family room. The whole idea is to be up, out of bed every day, and wheeling around the first floor and the deck. We and our dedicated in-home care team do that quite well now.

But no one who strolls into our kitchen would suggest that life on the Lane is even remotely monochromatic. The cabinet fronts and refrigerator doors are riots of color – mostly tempera paint, watercolor, and magic marker, part of a growing collection of original grandkid art. There are creations from at least four years, some from Thomas, 8, some from Archie, 6, and a few unsigned works likely from James, 6, and Leia, now 4. Olivia, who just turned 2, will add her work soon, perhaps when she visits in late January. We have been bowled over by the wonders of grandparenting and are looking forward to ever greater wonderment as the grandkids work their way through preteen and teen years onward to young adulthood.

During a Thanksgiving visit, granddaughter Leia made a stop-the-presses discovery that launched Opa on a new project. Exploring a stack of books under the family room coffee table, she pulled out a copy of *Struwwelpeter* [STREW-ful-pay-tur], a notorious children’s book written in 1844 by Dr. Heinrich Hoffmann, a German psychiatrist. The book was an immediate success in Europe and beyond. Opa’s great-great-grandfather Fritz Mueller (b. 1822) likely read it to his kids in Germany before the family emigrated, and dozens of Mueller households across four generations had copies in German or English or both. Opa hadn’t seen this heirloom copy since he was a first grader and wasn’t even aware it was in the house. It is now part of FrauErica.org, Opa’s online Mueller history project, in both [German](https://www.frauerica.org/Images/0043.php?refer=/Text/MarkAlliChristmas2024.php) and [English](https://www.frauerica.org/Images/0042.php?refer=/Text/MarkAlliChristmas2024.php).

*Struwwelpeter* is actually Part Two of a recent expansion of the *FrauErica.org* site. Part One, just getting underway in late 2023, is also now complete. The *Sängerbote* [ZENG-air-boat-eh] is Opa’s 178-page compendium of all 55 Mueller contributions to a German literary and musical quarterly published in St. Louis for five years (1913-17). The new *Sängerbote* project includes a photocopy of the original page, a transliteration of the old-style German type, and an English translation for each of seven essays and 48 works of poetry. The entire [Mueller *Sängerbote*](https://www.frauerica.org/Text/Sängerbote.php) output is now part of the *FrauErica.org* site.

While the Flying Oma Device makes mobility possible, we have also been expanding our digital communications. During our childhoods, international telephone calls were an extravagant, exotic, and impossibly expensive proposition, so we are still amazed at our free and frequent FaceTime video conversations with family in Madrid and Zoom sessions with McMillans at home and abroad. Those video interactions, together with visits from friends and relations, help keep our worldview wide open. May was a case in point, with several of Alli’s classmates and former hockey teammates stopping by during the Brown Class of 1974 50th Reunion Weekend, some bearing Brown alumni/ae swag, no less (thank you, classmates!).

Loaf number one of Christmas *Stollen* is already history, and there are ingredients on hand for loaf number two, likely this weekend. Alli’s habit of taping Christmas cards around the passthrough between the kitchen and family room is also well underway. Bit by bit the holiday season is making its way determinedly into the day-to-day here on the Lane. We hope that’s true for your abode as well. It’s been a rocky year for everyone, with more rockiness likely to come. With luck, peace on Earth and goodwill among everyone will find a way to persevere.

Hopefully, and with lots of love,

Alli and Mark  
  
Both now at [mark101nickel@gmail.com](mailto:mark101nickel@gmail.com)  
(401) 835-1913

title: Christmas 2025
summary: Annual Christmas letters get a mixed reception: tossed unread, browsed lightly, responded to. In the aggregate, though, they have some historical value, some bits of information about who’s doing what. So here’s the archive.
content:
{{image:3050}}

December 7, 2025

Dear Friends,

Christmas 2025 has brought a few welcome improvements to the holiday decorations here on the Lane. The lighting along the driveway is now statelier, with floodlights illuminating the silvery skeletons of our dogwoods, which are crowned by strings of tiny white bulbs.

Out on the deck overlooking the backyard, the effect is quite different. There is a blaze of color. Strings of lights outline the deck and climb up the bird-feeder pole outside Alli’s window, forming what looks like a giant multicolored shepherd’s crook. Alli loves it. But the clever thing is how the scene outside her window is managed. No dull clock-based timer for us this year. We have a new light-sensitive switch that determines the precise moment when dusk has fallen and turns on the lights for exactly eight hours. The time of ignition changes slightly by the day as Sun and Earth move toward the winter solstice and begin the long slog back. We feel earthy, connected in an almost atavistic way to natural rhythms. Exactly right for the season.

We’ve made other adjustments as well. Last year we moved our inside decorations from the living room to the family room, where Alli spent much of her mornings. This year the Hoyer lift and wheelchair are a bit too much to manage, so the lights and crèche will continue their trip down the hall to the addition, where Alli can enjoy them during the day. Alli still contends valiantly with MSA (multiple system atrophy). She remains positive, involved, happy to greet visitors both digitally and in person despite the progressive MSA problems. Speaking is now very difficult, but her perception and understanding are still strong. We also have very good in-home caregivers. Alli likes them and they like her a lot.

Yes, we have more free time these days but there is no shortage of projects. The Frau Erica site – a web-based history of the Fritz Mueller family in America, still growing with 1,323 relatives – has taken a big step forward. Since its inception, the site has included an English translation of Fritz’s emigration journal prepared decades ago by Mark’s uncle Herbert, but no one seemed to know what Herbert had used as the original German text. That has changed. Theresa Delaplain, wife of Herbert’s late son Robert, forwarded an old German photocopy she found among Robert’s papers. Mark went hunting on the web and found that the University of Indiana’s Lilly Library has a copy of Fritz’s “Little Book” in its permanent collection. Mark ordered a high-resolution digital copy and compared it to the photocopy Robert had inherited. It turns out that the Lilly Library doesn’t just have a copy. It has *the actual 1867 Mueller family heirloom* – an exact smudge-for-smudge match — given to the library in 2006 by one of Fritz’s great-great-grandsons.

And there is more news from the archive. In 1915, Opa Mueller’s reverie at the crib of his infant son Herbert was published in St. Louis by the *Sängerbote*, a German music and literature quarterly. A facsimile of that original German, with a transliteration to a modern typeface and an English translation, appeared on the site last year. Now there is a [musical score and an MP4 recording](https://www.frauerica.org/Text/Sängerbote1.php) of Opa’s *“An mein Kind,”* set by Cousin Robert Mueller and recorded live during his memorial concert at the University of Arkansas. A newly rebound copy of the *Sängerbote* – 20 quarterly issues, including 55 essays and poetic works by Mueller authors – is now part of the Frau Erica archive.

Yes, there will be at least one Stollen this year, although production will likely be timed to coincide with a visit from hungry afficionados Archie and Leia, who are well-acquainted with traditional Christmas bakery. The house is a wonderful place when grandchildren are in residence. Olivia, 3, here from Madrid for Thanksgiving, made frequent trips down the hall to visit Oma, sometimes with a parent and often just by herself.

We didn’t even get a week into December before our first hard freeze. Prognosticators in print media and online predict less snow this year but warn of colder than usual days and nights. Not much to be done about that beyond fires in the fireplace and maybe a bit of hot cocoa, but it does encourage the gardener to start thinking and planning for the coming season. It was a tragic and downhill year for fruit trees. Both Damson plum trees developed the dreaded black knot fungal disease and needed to be removed and burned. That makes six empty spaces out there with only two apples and a single stone-fruit tree – a nectarine – still standing. The consultant at Sav-A-Tree (an ironic name, considering) suggests adding a third apple variety and perhaps branching out into pears. There has also been some evident success with peach and apricot in our area, but the gardener is now officially gun-shy about stone fruit. We shall see.

Meanwhile, with the solstice only a couple weeks away, we are counting on our dusk-sensitive light switch to make the proper dusk-time adjustments, hoping that longer days might help stem the tide of distressing news flowing from Washington and elsewhere. With luck and determination, we hope to maintain a peaceful, positive and contemplative approach to whatever 2026 might bring our way. We hope it works out that way for you too.

With much love and best wishes,

Alli and Mark  
  
Reach us both at [mark101nickel@gmail.com](mailto:mark101nickel@gmail.com)  
(401) 835-1913

title: Obituary: Martin E. Mueller
summary: This obituary ran in the *Green Bay Press-Gazette*, Green Bay, WI, on January 15, 2003
content:
Martin E. Mueller, 79, Newville, Penn., passed away Wednesday, Jan. 8, 2003, in Pennsylvania where he has resided the latter part of his life. He was born on June 13, 1923, in Frazee, Minn., to Paul and Etta Mueller. Martin was a veteran of World Wall II, serving in the Army Signal Corps. He married Phyllis Mary Krause on August 29, 1948, in Elcho, Wis. He put in over 35 years with Kinney Shoes Corp. and at the height of his career he retired as a vice president. In his free time he enjoyed the outdoors, fishing, hunting, and spending time with his family.

In addition to his parents, he was preceded in death by his wife, Phyllis Mary Krause; sister, Ellie Schedler, and brothers Karl and Paul.

Survivors include his children, Linda (Roger) Heid, Marty (Sue) Mueller, Mark (Angel) Mueller, Judy Mueller; grandchildren Annie (Rick) Weber, Heather Mueller, Martin Mueller, Eric Johnson, Jennifer Mueller, Ashley Johnson, Richie Mueller; great-grandson Logan Jones; one sister, Ruth Lemkie.

His loved ones will sadly miss him and will remember the humor and laughter he shared with us.

Private memorial services were held at Marnocha Funeral Home, Pulaski, with burial in Elm Grove Cemetery, Laney.

title: Allison McKibbin’s engagement ring
summary: The diamond solitaire has been in the family for four generations — originally as an engagement ring, then as a solitaire necklace — and has resided in Minnesota, Europe, Rhode Island, and currently in Richmond, Virginia.
content:
[Allison McKibbin](https://www.frauerica.org/Images/0009.php?refer=$refer)
married Charles Henry (C.H.) Bigelow Jr. on August 26, 1911. She was 29; he was 45. On their engagement, C.H. presented Allison with a 1.4-carat diamond solitaire ring. He was a widower with three children aged 10 to 14: Donald (b.1896), Alida (b.1898), and Charles Henry III (b.1901). He and Allison had two children: Mary Allison (Molly, b.1919) and John (b.1920).

Their eldest son Donald and his wife Honor served in the U.S. Foreign Service, stationed in Bucharest, Tangier, Addis Ababa, Geneva and elsewhere. When World War II began, Donald and Honor placed their two sons — Lawrence and Roger — in New England schools. The boys lived with their grandparents in St. Paul, Minnesota, when school was out of session, and came to know their American cousins well. (Barbara D. McMillan, [*Lawrence Graham Bigelow: An Artist Sees for His Country*](https://www.frauerica.org/Images/_Image.php?refer=$refer&image=0474) (Outskirts Press, Inc.; 2019))

Larry and Roger remained close with their grandparents. Soon after C.H. died in 1945, Allison gave the diamond solitaire ring to Larry as a keepsake. Larry, a talented artist who also served in the OSS during the war and later in the CIA, devoted himself to painting, with shows at the Betty Parsons Gallery in New York and at galleries in Washington, Dublin, and London. A few years after he and his wife Franka had settled in Rolle, Switzerland, on the shore of Lake Geneva, Larry sent the diamond ring to his cousin Molly Bigelow McMillan with a note saying “back home to family again, with affectionate wishes, Larry.” His cousin Rick and wife Barbara, carried the ring back to Minnesota after a visit with Larry and Franka.

Molly — Nana to her grandchildren and great-grandchildren — decided to have the diamond reset as a solitaire necklace for her daughter Allison. Her handwritten note on an appraisal report: “Given to Allison McMillan on the occasion of her marriage to Mark Nickel, July 26, 1986.”

A little more than 30 years later, more than a century after Allison McKibbin had first received it, Allison McMillan presented it to her daughter Susan Allison Crawley. Susan later had it reset in its original state as a ring.

**About the stone**  
Molly McMillan had the stone appraised in 1986 before giving it to her daughter Allison. It is an Old European cut, a style popular from 1890 to about 1930, said to emphasize “fire” (dispersion of light from within) rather than the modern “sparkle” (brilliant black-and-white patterns across the surface). Old European diamonds, all hand-cut, have more quirks than modern machine cuts; each is unique. The stone was rated “nearly colorless” (2 on a 10-point color scale) with some inclusions but none visible without a microscope (6 on the clarity scale). The appraisal grade was provisional because the stone was mounted. Full AGS grading (a zero ranking is highest) requires unmounted stones. (Barbara D. McMillan, [*Lawrence Graham Bigelow: An Artist Sees for His Country*](https://www.frauerica.org/Images/_Image.php?refer=$refer&image=0474) (Outskirts Press, Inc.; 2019))

title: Otto Mueller on the original Mueller farm
summary: Otto was about 8 years old when when his parents sold their small farm at Story Lake, near Kendallville, and acquired the original Mueller farm. Otto grew up to become a Lutheran minister. He is buried in the churchyard of St. John’s in Kendallville. This comes from an extensive memoir Otto wrote in retirement.
content:
After seven years at Story Lake, my parents sold their farm and acquired the old Mueller homestead. I well remember the day of moving, April 1, 1890. Uncles and neighbors came with wagons. Roads at that time were neither graded nor graveled, and in the early spring were in really bad shape. First of all, my sister Emma was started out with a flock of sheep. She was twelve years old at the time and knew how to direct the flock. When the wagons reached their destination later in the afternoon, the young shepherdess had already arrived, justly proud of having covered the seven long miles on foot.

It was at this old Mueller estate that I spent my formative years. The house was old, consisting of a main part, two stories and a cellar, and a long stretch of one-story rooms. My parents soon had one part of the house moved a short distance away, which then served as a summer kitchen and woodhouse.

The old saw mill had been converted into a barn. There were huge square beams, two feet each way, fifty to sixty feet long. The building was not well-suited for a barn, but my mother, coming from that old log barn at Story Lake, often remarked how nice it was to have room for stock and feed with a good roof overhead.

Then there was a so-called horse barn farther up the hill. Father usually took care of the horses while we two boys chored in the big barn tending cattle and sheep, of which we always had quite a flock.

The old farm, which had been rented out for some years, was badly rundown. All the rich lowland was overgrown with trees and brush. Only the hills were being farmed, and some of those were badly eroded. My father undertook the task of draining and clearing the marshes. Isalah and Amos Wert and Henry Hemer did much ditching and tiling. The ash trees were cut into four-foot lengths and sawn into pickets, with which many rods of fences were built. It took years until all the stumps were burned or rotted away. The soil, however, was rich and produced good crops of corn and potatoes. Clover was grown on the high ground. The second crop was left for seed, of which we sometimes threshed quite a few bushels. For five years, while my brother and I still attended school, one of our cousins from Wisconsin, Enno Boese, served as a hired man. His pet saying was, “When I get a farm, I fix me it.”

title:
My Life and Its Times  

Mary Allison (Molly) Bigelow McMillan
summary: Molly McMillan — Nana, to her grandchildren and great-grandchildren — had a lot to distill into a memoir: daughter of a long-established Twin Cities family, physics graduate of Vassar, war-time bride, early feminist, civic leader, among the first ordained Presbyterian women in Minnesota, mother of five, grandmother of 15, great-grandmother of 10. She worked on the following chapters for several years, signing and dating the memoir on All Saints Day, November 1, 2007. Life — and great-grandchildren — kept coming for nearly another decade.
content:
“Our parents give us their genes as well as what they share with us in training and experiences. We may want to reject such remembrances, but it’s wiser to accept them for better or for worse and for what we may gain in self- understanding. The genes we can’t do anything about.”

“Father’s exercise was rowing. One time he rowed with me — I was too little to help — all the way to Wildwood, where we stopped in a little park to the east of the amusement complex and he read to me from the Brownie Book. And of course he rowed back, close to five miles round trip.”

“[Father’s] other love was the woods across the road from our house that he also owned. Almost every night he would take his sickle and trim the paths that he had made, and we would walk along them, the first one in line breaking the spider webs.”

“It was in the world of ideas that my Mother lived. When I visualize her, she is sitting at her desk. Other daughters may have learned to sew or cook from their mothers. I learned to write letters, to use the vertical pronoun as little as possible. She did, however, teach me to knit, a talent I have valued for over seventy years.”

“As soon as school was out we moved out to Manitou Island. Invariably, no matter what the date, it always seemed that we would have two weeks of cold and rain. Such is the impatience of youth. In the 1920s and ’30s the island was a summer playground for its many children.”

“I was the only [physics] major in my class. ... What I understood about physics was what I could not see — atoms and molecules and the like. What I could not manage were the formulas required for a ball rolling down an inclined plane. ... I took singing lessons all four Vassar years, but not for credit.”

“Our children know how blessed they were to have their father. He had grown up with children living next door who loved him. He was always ready for some play. He would have been happy to have a dozen of his own. It was his parenting that grounded the family.”

“It reminded me of another nest brought down by a strong wind. When Ricky and Charley and I went out after the storm to look around, there it was, still with its soft interior *plus* a baby in the nest.”

“It was a heady time for me. I was the expert from out of town evaluating the [Junior] Leagues in my area. I was meeting and becoming friends with women from all over the country. I was also learning about nonprofit board-staff relationships and how they should be handled.”

“As I was completing my term on the [Association of Junior Leagues of America] Board, Mayor Vavoulis of St. Paul needed a replacement for the woman member of the Ramsey County Welfare Board. Charlie Birt of the Community Chest suggested my name.”

“God was always a reality in my life. Sitting in an airplane in the days when there was something to look at out the window, I would contemplate the nature of God, the all-seeing and all-knowing. Once there was rainbow, a full circle with the shadow of the airplane in the middle. It was covanental.”

“And when I put my pencil down to my notebook to begin to record, it suddenly took off on its own and wrote, ‘My way comes through Thee, O God.’ Such joy!”

“The seminary had the wholehearted support of Dick. The McMillan Administration building honors that support. Later on, after I had sold both the Arizona and Manitou Island houses, I was able to give the initial gift for the very beautiful Bigelow Chapel.”

“A year ago I moved. But the trees that I left are in my mind’s eye. To that elm, to those oaks and maples, I can now add all the ones that are no longer there in this time and in that space.”

“After a year of testing, I acted on Dick’s wish that the Arizona house be sold and came home for good. I love fresh snow, and driving in the winter for a Minnesotan is like riding a bicycle — you never forget how.”

“I woke one morning saying the first three lines, so I wrote them down to see what would come next.”

“Grandchildren getting married, great-grandchildren arriving make for a joyful future. I have always said that I would live to be a hundred, since that is the next numbers bend. But I try to live each day as if it might be the last and have no fear of going to the other side still surrounded by God’s love.

Molly McMillan  
All Saints Day, 2007

Text to come

title: Foreword
summary: Molly”s forebears — Bigelows, McKibbins, Dorseys, Dorrises — were English or Scots-Irish. They arrived in St. Paul from New York, Massachusetts, and Pennsylvania in the mid-19th century — and they thrived.
content:
My theology, that is my understanding of human relationships with God and with each other, requires that I start this memoir with some mention of my mother and father. We carry the past in some form with us as we enter the present and go forward into the future. Our parents give us their genes as well as what they share with us in training and experiences. We may want to reject such remembrances, but it’s wiser to accept them for better or for worse and for what we may gain in self- understanding. The genes we can’t do anything about.

All my grandparents arrived in St. Paul, Minnesota, in the 1860s and ‘70s. The Bigelows were of English stock. Charles Henry Bigelow was born in Easton, New York. His future wife, Alida Lyman, was born not far away in Chester, Massachusetts. They set up housekeeping in Troy, New York, but began their trek west after the great fire in Troy in about 1862, from which they escaped with only the clothes on their backs, their baby Emma, and the family Bible. My father, Charles Henry Bigelow Jr., was born on June 4, 1866, shortly after the family arrived in St. Paul.

The McKibbins, Dorseys, and Dorrises were Scots-Irish from County Antrim and County Down and came to St. Paul via Pennsylvania. My grandfather, Joseph McKibbin, arrived because his brother, William, was pastor of Central Presbyterian Church in St. Paul. Visiting Ireland some years ago on a golfing vacation I found a book of ballads, one of which talked about McKibbins as great sailors. When I spoke of this to my caddie, he said, “They still are! And big boats, too.” (The same caddie told me that McMillans, no matter how they spell their names, are always Scots.)

Joseph met Annie Dorsey in St. Paul. My mother, Allison, was born on September 14, 1881. In 1884, Annie died from complications of childbirth. Joseph married Annie’s older sister, Mary [Mary Henderson Dorsey], in 1885 and she was the one I knew as grandmother and was named for, although Allison was substituted for Henderson. From her also I received my nickname of Molly. It was a name she liked.

The net result of all this is that I am a WASP, White Anglo-Saxon Protestant. The reader shall be the judge of whether I have lived up to or lived down that designation.

The reader will also discover that I have remained connected throughout my life to the House of Hope Presbyterian Church in St. Paul into which I was baptized as an infant.

title: Back Home
summary: Molly’s experience as a regional officer for the Junior League prepared her for positions of community leadership in St. Paul and Ramsey County as well as for service on various foundation boards.
content:
As I was completing my term on the AJLA Board, Mayor Vavoulis of St. Paul needed a replacement for the woman member of the Ramsey County Welfare Board. Charlie Birt of the Community Chest suggested my name.

This appointed Board had come about because an earlier Board of Control administering the County Home, Ancker Hospital and Outdoor Relief had been found guilty of fraud in 1928. The new Board of Public Welfare was to consist of five citizens having no other political office or appointment.

In 1937, in response to increasing federal and state legislation in the welfare field only the name of the board was changed. Its makeup remained the same. That its original members were those already involved with the private agencies interested in the welfare of the less fortunate of the city was instrumental in the strong public/private cooperation that has always been the norm in St. Paul. I offered a full discussion of this in a paper written for the New Century Club in 1967. A copy is on file at the Minnesota Historical Society.

The Welfare Board met weekly and we were paid $300 a month. One of my cousins said to me, “You are going to give that money away, aren’t you?” Well, no. I might give an equivalent away, but being paid was a pleasure. We backed a very fine and thoughtful staff and worked through many important decisions. Mother’s stories of little Matilda gave me understanding. In addition my aunt, Margaret McKibbin, had worked for the welfare department caring for the children of unwed mothers. Her stories of unrealistic restrictions had also impressed.

During my time on the Board, we built a new hospital to replace the old Ancker. It is now called Regions, continues as a fine teaching hospital, and is suitably enlarged.

Under a grant from the federal government, the staff of the Welfare Board set up a work and training program for long term clients. In this program we found that many clients had serious health problems, and that training had to start at the level of cleanliness and promptness. The program was a great success. As I said in my 1967 paper, “Some of the most famous names in welfare have been missing for as much as three years.” Unfortunately the grant was not renewed. Similar grants in other parts of the country had been fraudulently used.

In 1967, the staff thought that counseling related to family planning should be considered. A committee, which I chaired, was set up with representatives from the various agencies in town. When the time finally came for a vote, it was unanimous in favor of the new counseling. I remember looking at the Executive Director representing Catholic Charities and he nodded his head. I’ve wondered whether his acquiescence had anything to do with his move to a parish shortly thereafter. To this day Catholics are not officially in favor of birth control.

A change came in the Mayor’s office. Larry Byrne was a Democrat and a Catholic. I was replaced, learning of it first from our very fine reporter for the Pioneer Press who always attended our meetings. I have kept a number of letters of appreciation from different members of the staff. A few years later, in the early ‘70’s the law was changed to have the Board elected by the citizens of Ramsey County.

Next came a call from the Community Chest to head up the Residential Division of the annual campaign. The goal included an increase over the previous year of 7%. But as I was careful to explain to the solicitors who would be calling on larger givers, the increase that a person who has given $100 is much more likely to be $110 or even $125, each well over the 7%.

As we approached the end of the campaign I found myself able to make some cold calls to some of the larger givers asking for an additional amount. Many responded positively. So came the night to report. Instead of padding some of the divisions with extra funds held back, the Community Chest staff had decided to let the chips fall. The Residential Division at 103.4% was the only one to go over goal. We whooped and hollered!

What next? I was serving on the Family Service Board and it seemed to make sense to set up a Home Service Agency, under the aegis of the Community Chest. The care of the elderly or disabled in their own homes was far cheaper than in a hospital. We hired an executive, I helped with setting up the books and chaired the Board, but Grace Gumnit was the angel of the endeavor. She knew how to negotiate contracts with the Welfare Department. At one point a Community Chest staff member told me that Home Services was broke. It seems they had never heard of Accounts Receivable. I have some regrets that I did not follow through with Home Services as I should have, but I was headed for Seminary. They eventually merged with a larger organization.

In 1973 I was asked to join the Wilder Foundation Board, its first woman member. This was a signal honor as Wilder was the second largest operating foundation in the country. (I turned down a similar offer of “first” from the St. Paul Fire and Marine Insurance Company. My interest was not in business.) On the Wilder Board I found that what I had learned about board – staff relationships, while I was on the AJLA Board, was appreciated by Leonard Wilkening, the CEO. A seven persons board, these giants in St. Paul industry with whom I was now associated, had a natural inclination to micromanage. Since we had the funds to hire first rate employees, we encouraged them to take on the difficult issues confronting the east metro area.

Two major decisions took place during my years on the Wilder Board: one reached reluctantly, the board overruling the administration, the other revitalizing a whole area of the city, a project of great imagination.

The first was the necessity of tearing down the Wilder Building on the corner of Fifth and Washington in order to build the Ordway Theater. The Wilder building had housed all the non-profit agencies for many years. To tear it down would cause a major disruption in their lives. Leonard had a difficult time with this. Since I was not of the business community, I sided with Leonard for a long time before finally voting in favor of the move.

The second was the development of Energy Park and Bandana Square. It includes housing as well as office space, a medical clinic and hotel, and is a vibrant replacement for what were old railroad barns. As I go today to see my doctor in Energy Park I continue to marvel at what Wilder accomplished.

And then I had the pleasure of going on the Board of the F. R. Bigelow Foundation, chaired by my cousin, Carl B. Drake, Jr. It was a nice change to be able to give away money. In self-help initiatives that we sponsored, we found that what we thought was needed was not always what those involved thought were needed. The powerful serve best when they listen with humility.

Age limitation requirements caused my resignation from both boards, but by that time I was well into the administration struggles of United Seminary.

title: Road to Ministry
summary: Molly had returned to the House of Hope after college. Her experience there deepened, including election as an elder and a seminal study group of churchwomen. Studies at United Theological Seminary, originally as part of a graduate program, deepened as well.
content:
Several themes or understandings emerge in my life that have been fairly constant.

The theology I am most comfortable with is the one called process. It comes out of attempts to connect the spiritual and the scientific. Its most important idea is that every moment is a complete moment. Each of these moments contains our past history, the context in which we find ourselves, and God who, in the wonderful words of Paul’s letter to the Philippians, “works in you, inspiring both the will and the deed, for God’s chosen purpose.” (2:13 NEB)

On a more personal level, I have an inherited native ability for administration. I can tell people how to do something, and even encourage them in it, much better than I can do it myself!

Presbyterians are not graced at baptism with godmothers and godfathers, but as a child I had a godmother. That is what we called my father’s first wife, Florence. And I had the Father God of the Lord’s Prayer. Since I knew all about very much older brothers as well as a sister, it was not hard to think of Jesus as my brother also. He was the one who told all those wonderful stories about finding lost sheep, welcoming back prodigal sons and being Good Samaritan neighbors. John and I were well grounded in the Bible.

God was always a reality in my life. Sitting in an airplane in the days when there was something to look at out the window I would contemplate the nature of God, the all-seeing and all knowing. Once there was rainbow, a full circle with the shadow of the airplane in the middle. It was covenantal.

The activities of the worship service always interested me. I had helped my father figure out how to fold the Communion cloth so that it could easily be put back on the table at the end of the service. At college I was active in the work of the chapel and was in charge of the daily services my senior year. I prepared and served Communion. Intuitively I understood that well-ordered worship is in itself an act of worship.

I was in the choir and loved it, but we did not go to youth activities. Nor did our children. They attended Sunday School and church and that was it.

A few years after returning to the community, I was encouraged to get started with the Women’s Association of the House of Hope. Of course it was not long before I ended up as president. We were doing some studying with professors from Macalester College: the great theologian, Paul Tillich, the unusual psychologist, Carl Jung, and the Biblical letters of Paul.

In addition a small group of us began to meet with a psychologist from the university. His interest was in seeing how long it would take a group of churchwomen to stop intellectualizing and to say something personal about their religious understanding. It was a nine weeks session and it took us seven weeks! Those last three weeks were electrifying. From that group three of us went on to seminary, two of us getting degrees. A fourth member was moved to enter into a different kind of work. The group added some members and went on for a second session, but I don’t even remember what happened during that time!

As for me, it was a time of some crisis. My mother had died and as I held her hand her life force, her love, came through into my arm and I felt a most enormous sense of peace. She had been a student of the Bible and of theology. I had not. She had confessed Jesus as the Christ and loved the Gospel of John. My Jesus was of the synoptic gospels, and I considered John’s gospel to be extravagant writing. Mother had been ill for a number of years, so you might think this peace that I felt represented a sense of relief in me, but it was an entirely different sensation. For me it represents the “peace that passes understanding.”

Before Mother’s long last illness we had had a number of discussions about Jesus. I could not share her belief in his divinity. I did share her belief, however, that the other side is not far away. She had felt Florence’s closeness in the years she was bringing up my older brothers and sister. And I had already come to an understanding that God was Love itself, not just loving. So I came to a moment when I took a cue from Paul, who did not distinguish his Damascus road experience from the appearances of Jesus to the other disciples. The empty tomb stories were overlay, I felt, to emphasize that Jesus was truly human and not just a divine spirit — but the appearances of one who is totally loving and forgiving were real. On my knees I confessed that, although I did not understand, I knew that Jesus was the Christ, the one chosen to show us that we are unfailingly loved, unfailingly forgiven.

That night God became personal for me. I awoke to a feeling of weightlessness and the words “communion of saints.” That was my mother’s favorite phrase, and so I believe that it was my mother who came to me and lifted me up. We were finally in sync.

In the morning I discovered that the ambiguities and paradoxes in Tillich’s sermons were quite clear, and that Paul’s letter to the Romans and the Gospel of John, neither of which I had ever understood, were “easy.” I looked at my violet in the east window and believed that its flowers glistening in the sunshine were the most beautiful things I had ever seen. And when I put my pencil down to my notebook to begin to record, it suddenly took off on its own and wrote, “My way comes through Thee, O God.” Such joy! Joy is much more than happiness. It is almost physical in its penetrating and surrounding quality. I wonder if it was joy that the disciples who had all run away felt when their Lord appeared to them. Joy doesn’t last, but it returns occasionally, and the memory of it undergirds daily events.

There were other side effects, a most uncharacteristic docile following of Dick, a new appreciation of myself as a woman, and a critical awareness that some good works are what I now call “do-gooding” because the ego is involved. To do good is of a different order. The depth and mystery in services of worship and the sacraments also came alive. In the past I had been bothered that the House of Hope did not do enough in the community. Now I realized that worship itself was an offering to God and that the social gospel was only half the loaf.

These surprising events took study. I did not want to pursue automatic writing, but have wondered if it has a similarity for those who think with their pens, as I do, to glossolalia, the traditional speaking in tongues. Reading William James and other books on psychology reassured me that though these happenings were unusual they were by no means unique.

It seems to me that we are cared for by God and by that Communion of Saints which has gone before. God is Love, not loving — though that too — but Love. And Love is an energy similar to electricity. It is powerful but does not show itself without connectedness. I have also come to picture God as a parent, always loving but always bemused by humanity’s goings-on, both saddened and rejoicing. The whole Biblical witness shows God’s steadfast love for us and an overwhelming desire for us to live in community with one another. But it saddens me that the expression “born again” has come to be associated with a return to inerrancy, a literalist reading of the Bible. That is not what Jesus was talking about.

It was my friend, June Kootz, who pushed me toward seminary training. She was active in the United Church of Christ and knew about United Seminary of the Twin Cities located in New Brighton just north of St. Paul and Minneapolis.. After I had discussed all this with her, and also my discovery that Dayton Hultgren, the incoming president, had his doctorate in the field of adult psychology, she picked up the phone and asked the seminary to send me a catalog.

But I had no call to the ministry. I went simply to study — for a Master of Arts degree, yes, because I knew myself well enough to know that writing exams and papers was essential to my learning.

In the meantime I had been elected an elder at House of Hope and had served as clerk of session. I had also been working at the presbytery level. I helped write by-laws for the Presbytery of St. Paul, for the Presbytery of the Twin Cities when we merged with Minneapolis, and for the Presbytery of the Twin Cities Area when we took in other churches in southern Minnesota and western Wisconsin. This is also as good a time as any to say that I have served on Presbytery Council, had two terms on the Permanent Judicial Commission, and have served on a number of other committees. Much later, in 1986, I was chair of the Local Arrangements Committee for the General Assembly meeting in the Twin Cities. That was a two-year job with 2,000 volunteers and a paid staff. I was a commissioner to the Biloxi General Assembly in 1987, and served as a committee assistant at the Milwaukee General Assembly in 1988 or 1989.

United Seminary was, and is, an extraordinary place. I was older and richer than the other students, but was totally accepted. I also found that my understanding of the Bible was better than most and that Presbyterian polity was incomprehensible to most. The Master of Arts degree was not as rigorous as it is now and could easily have been accomplished, but I found that I was troubled.

The 1970s were when Episcopalian women were trying to become priests. After two years, other women at the seminary wondered out loud why they were trying to become ministers and I was not. There did not seem much likelihood in that, but the idea caused enough disturbance in my mind to make me change my degree program, and presume that passing my “trials for ordination” would bring this adventure to a successful conclusion. Dick’s business in St. Paul was going well, so I was not particularly free to pursue calls even if it had entered my head.

The House of Hope was between assistant ministers so I asked if I could intern there. (To intern in your home church is no longer allowed.). Those seven months were a good experience. I found that I could manage as a professional, and Dick was comfortable with the arrangement. We enjoyed arriving home about the same time and jointly fixing dinner. And it was 1976, the year of the Bicentennial so, with Marilyn Noah Loftsgaarden’s able help, we planned the first and largest of the Ice Cream Socials, closing off Portland and filling the parking lot.

United requires continuing assessment in the Career Development Center and also one unit of Pastoral Counseling in a hospital setting. From these I gained confidence in the fact that I might be suited for ministry, but that it should not take the form of hospital chaplaincy. There were no measures for women ministers at that time but I went off the scale as an executive secretary. Therefore on I went to prepare for my trials for ordination without expectation.

What we identify as “a call” is rarely a zap from God. It is a building up of a conviction from a variety of sources. What a humbling and heady experience to take my place on the staff of my home church. My internship had served as my introduction. When Mr. Didier offered two names to the Personnel Committee, I was chosen. The older women in the congregation were quite frank to tell me that they didn’t think much of the idea! At least they admired my voice and my reading of Scripture.

I was ordained on June 11, 1978. Although I had been earlier ordained as an elder, this had a different feel to it. I remember the weight of the hands and also the unexpectedness of the mike put in front of me as I was kneeling so that I might offer a prayer. I do not remember exactly what I said, but I surely asked for God’s help.

My interest in the study of the Bible got its start because Cal Didier, the Pastor, used his wide knowledge of literature and poetry in his sermons. The other assistant, Cam McConnell, preached athletic sermons, and so I thought my niche could be the Biblical.

I shall always be grateful to Cal Didier and to the members of the Personnel Committee who called me. And I shall particularly remember Clarence Hautzenrader, a retired Disciples of Christ minister who was the visitation pastor. Clarence taught me so much about the art of ministry. He taught me how to call on all our members at the Presbyterian Homes in one afternoon. He also taught me how to receive assorted odd objects like snowballs or pancakes that might come up to the Communion Table in the offering plates from fun-loving deacons. Snowballs go in the flower arrangement. Pancakes are palmed for later disposal.

I was one of the first six or seven women to be ordained in the Presbytery of the Twin Cities Area. I think my age contributed to my having an easier time of it than some. Women’s styles of leadership are different from men’s. We are more horizontally relational than hierarchical, and that is an attitude that was not at that time always easily translated into the work of ministry. I gave a paper on this to the New Century Club a number of years ago that was picked up by the Reorganized Latter Day Saints in Independence Missouri for publication in their *Saints Herald*. They were about to ordain a whole lot of women at one time.

While speaking of publishing, I also am the co-author with Dayton Hultgren, of a chapter on the choosing of trustees for a book called *The Good Steward*. The book has to do with the management of seminaries. Dayton’s idea, novel at the time, was that one of the attributes of a trustee of a seminary should be their acknowledgement that they were on a spiritual journey just as were the students and faculty. (And then there’s the *History of Manitou Island*, co-authored with fellow resident Sue Wolsfeld.)

The third summer at House of Hope I spent a good part of the summer in total charge due to the vagaries of vacations. I preached five Sundays in a row and realized that I still had things to say. I officiated at several funerals with good reviews and hosted the visiting minister from St. Giles Cathedral in Edinburgh, Scotland. In addition, we formed a committee and helped the Dayton Avenue Presbyterian Church avoid having its condemned steeple torn down.

There was no comment on any of this when the staff returned in the fall. In addition, Cal and I were in serious disagreement about Presbytery. He wanted nothing to do with it. I was working hard for it. It seemed that it was time to leave. Though I did not know what I would do next, I knew that the seminary, where I was by now a trustee, could use some help in fundraising. February 1, 1982, would be my leaving date.

series_title: My Way Comes Through Thee, O God
title: My Way Through Thee
summary: In her section on “The Unlikely Road to Ministry,” Molly describes a night of contemplation and spiritual awakening. When she took up her pen the next morning, she felt an almost automatic jotting of the phrase, “My way comes through thee, O God.” This is the poem she created then.

series_title: United Theological Seminary of the Twin Cities
title: The Seminary
summary: Ordained and well-experienced in leading a large congregation, Molly returned to United Theological Seminary as interim president, then chair of the Board, and finally as fundraiser and patron for Bigelow Chapel.
content:
United was formed by the merger of an evangelical and reformed seminary in Lakeland, Wisconsin, and a congregational one in Yankton, South Dakota. Its charter was to be ecumenical, welcoming a variety of faiths, and it has always taken that charge seriously. As a result we learned about each other’s denominations. In many cases its graduates were able break down prejudices between churches in the towns in which they served. I have spoken of feeling accepted there. It was an example of the new creation in Christ of which Paul wrote. The “real world” was outside its walls.

Dick and I were in Florida when the chair of the Board of United Seminary called one day and said that the president, Dayton Hultgren, was leaving to become president of San Francisco Seminary, and that he wanted to come down and talk to me about my becoming interim president. I told him to come on down and we could talk together about who else should be interim president. I am not a self-starter. It has been the history of my life that doors have opened up, and I have trusted enough to be willing to walk through them. And so I became interim president. What a gift to me that year was. All the skills I had developed over the years were put to use, and the faculty were exceptionally generous in their acceptance of my leadership.

On July 1, 1982, Jo Usher, a brusque but brilliant staff member, took me in hand. We let an inadequate secretary go, and Jo became my assistant. While the first order of business was to set up a search committee for a new president, the next problem was the Center for the Shaping of Values, to which I had given startup funds when Dayton was trying to raise money from those who would not give to religious organizations. The Center, due to a variety of circumstances, had not worked out as planned and was operating almost as a separate entity. It was not until Mary Bednarowski became interim dean in the second semester that we were able to close the Center. Our successful businessmen husbands encouraged us, and a faculty committee under the leadership of Lance Barker affirmed the rationale.

The finances of the seminary were shaky. A suburban newspaper ran a headline, “Can she save the Seminary?” The banker thought we should sue. I thought we should be glad that only a few had read the article. Fortunately Dayton had begun the work of bringing Twin Cities residents on the Board, and so we were able to solicit some funds. We also realized that we had property that was more than was needed for the seminary enterprise. Professors were beginning to prefer living off campus rather than in the homes that had been provided for them. Jo dealt with city fathers and the process of selling off began.

The Presidential Search Committee worked hard that year, calling Howard Mills as Dayton’s successor. On July 1, 1983, I went back to being a Board member. In 1986 I became chair of the Board, retiring in 1989. At that time I was graciously awarded the honorary Doctor of Divinity degree. And sometime in these years my name was proposed to Marquis *Who’s Who in America*.

The seminary had the wholehearted support of Dick. The McMillan Administration Building honors that support. Later on, after I had sold both the Arizona and Manitou Island Houses, I was able to give the initial gift for the very beautiful Bigelow Chapel. It and the additions to the library, which Ed and Harriet Spencer have generously provided, complete the campus grounds. Still needed is endowment and scholarship support.

It was during this interim president year that I was invited to go on the board of the Ecumenical and Cultural Institute housed on the St. John’s Monastery and University campus at Collegeville, Minnesota. The Collegeville Institute is devoted to building bridges between denominations through housing scholars for their sabbatical studies and offering summer workshops on different faith issues. One memorable workshop was on prayer. The participants worked at getting a mythical man name Joe buried. They found that they could not accomplish the task. Prayer, it seems, has many different meanings and purposes.

One of the givens for the workshops and conversations between scholars is the use of the personal pronoun. There is no hiding behind theoretical knowledge. And it is through the personal that deep friendships can take root. I am reminded of the long seven weeks it had taken the House of Hope Women’s group to break through to something important.

The Benedictine Communities of St. John’s and its sister school St. Benedict’s in nearby St. Joseph offer a hospitality of spirit that overrides the strict dictates of Roman Catholicism. To visit is peace-filling. I have enjoyed a long association with the Institute and am now an honorary member of the board.

title: Trees
summary: Trees were important to Molly, both as trees and as metaphors for grouping and fixing memories. When she wrote about her father, she wrote also about [the woods](NanaLifeTimes3.php). She wrote “Trees” at another turning point in her life, when she left Manitou Island and moved to her apartment at EagleCrest.

title: The Years Alone
summary: Five years after her husband died, Molly sold the home on Manitou Island and moved to an apartment in EagleCrest, a Presbyterian Home in Roseville, Minnesota. She traveled, continued to do “supply preaching” in congregations with vacancies, and found ways to further pursue some of the intellectual and theological questions that intrigued her.
content:
Dick died on June 15, 1995, collapsing on the street as he was running on the Island before going out to work in Woodville. We had spent a wonderful year in Carefree, Arizona, where we had bought a house and redecorated it with the help of John Austin. That was a very special year for us, a year of great happiness together, a year for which I shall always be grateful. However, in the spring we had gone through the Scottsdale Mayo Clinic. Dick received a diagnosis of increasing diabetic senility. He already knew that his golfing ability was impaired, but he lacked the judgment to slow up as he was running up the hill towards home on that final day.

His ashes are buried in the Garth at House of Hope. Some day mine will be there also, and my name will be added to his on the stone on the north wall. The memorial service with the choir singing and Doug giving a beautiful remembrance of his father was a true witness to the resurrection and to Dick’s life and to his integrity.

After a year of testing, I acted on Dick’s wish that the Arizona house be sold and came home for good. I love fresh snow, and driving in the winter for a Minnesotan is like riding a bicycle — you never forget how.

In July after Dick’s death, Jim Carter, House of Hope’s senior pastor, and Tom Lancaster, the choir director came to see me, and told me that Stephen Paulus and Michael Dennis Browne were eager to do an opera based on Leo Tolstoy’s story, *The Three Hermits*. I immediately agreed to fund it, knowing that the children would help, as it was a perfect memorial to Dick.

What intrigued me about the story was that it was about an arrogance of power which the Bishop was showing, and which I recognized. When I had volunteered in a number of St. Paul community agencies, I had seen that arrogance in operation. With the best will in the world, those holding the purse strings assumed that they knew what was best for those whom they presumed to serve. In fact, I wrote my first paper after entering seminary about this issue in relation to the United Way — an agency, incidentally, which I heartily support.

When Michael Dennis Browne began discussing the addition of women’s voices to the opera, I fell into the same trap myself. I assumed that the leading female role would be that of a Mother Superior! Michael, so much wiser, knew that the Bishop should have a mother who would both love and humorously needle her son. Michael has created a marvelous bit of dialog where the Bishop’s only response is that of children everywhere, “Oh, mother — hush!” That I was allowed to sit I on these discussions was a great gift.

Thanks to Gary Gisselman’s direction, there is great humor in this production, particularly when the Bishop attempts to improve the hermits’ prayer life by teaching them the Lord’s Prayer. (One production that I saw on the East Coast, however, offered no humor at all.) This is a church opera with a deeply spiritual component, but hearty laughter is encouraged. I’m quite sure that God is joining in.

House of Hope presented the opera in both 1997 and 2001. The final eight parts chorus, “The Pilgrims’ Hymn,” is Stephen’s most popular work. He graciously dedicated it to me. After a few years, Allison prevailed on him to publish a version for four parts suitable for smaller choirs. And recently “The Pilgrims’ Hymn” was chosen by the University of Minnesota for its Wall of Discovery that honors its distinguished alums and faculty. There, in honor of Stephen and Michael, the first page sits, right along with obscure mathematical formulae and discoveries leading to better tasting apples.

Grief is a tricky emotion. I preached without a problem two weeks after Dick died. It took longer before I was able to write thank-you notes for the many expressions of love and admiration for Dick. And I felt unable to carry on the tradition that we had had together of inviting our grandchildren at the age of ten for a solo visit. I would tour with them in the daytime, and Dick would take them fishing in the evening. Thus I missed an opportunity to get better acquainted with Doug’s children. Anne’s children from Spain also missed out.

These years alone have been enhanced with many trips: Italy with Harriet Gibbs Gardiner, Turkey with a group from House of Hope, Linn and Lois Glover Creighton joining us. Polly Shank and I adventured in two directions: up the Volga to Moscow from Volgograd (Stalingrad to old-timers), and the Scottish Highlands and Islands. Ruth Brin and I had a wonderful elder hostel trip to South Africa. We also took a driving tour of the national parks in Utah, picking up our car in Las Vegas and returning it in Denver. Another House of Hope trip to the British Isles found us marooned on the island of Iona because of high winds and the overly enthusiastic previous night’s celebration by the ferryman.

For several years I enjoyed supply preaching in a number of different churches, although I realize now that recycled urban sermons were not always apropos in the more rural churches. Congregations, however, were always polite. The time came when it was no longer easy to lead an entire service. Facilitating Bible study became the answer to what I feel is my call to teach/preach. At first this was on Sunday mornings between the two services at House of Hope. Now we study the lectionary texts on Wednesdays with a devoted and loving group of retirees. And in 2007 I was able to interest a group at EagleCrest in “Bible study using recent research.” We call ourselves the Thursday Study group.

EagleCrest Terrace, a Presbyterian Homes independent living retirement community in Roseville, just north of St. Paul,, has been my home since September 2001. Docks, boats, leaky gutters became impediments to my life. What I miss most is the water and approaching storms. My apartment, lovely as it is, faces east. I did not know anyone living at EagleCrest when I moved in, but my friend Anne Elsinger had taught me so much about the game of bridge that I knew I would be comfortable accepting an invitation to play. And play I do, regularly.

The Terrace was built by Northwestern College where Billy Graham, I believe, commenced his ministry. Presbyterian Homes and Services took over the management and later its ownership a few years later. At the time I moved in its culture was heavily Baptist and Free Evangelical. One day I met Ted Nordquist, the seventh floor representative to the resident council who had greeted me when I moved in, at the mailbox and said, “I’ve just given a paper (at the New Century Club) on the Gospel of John and I don’t think they understood a single word of it.” Ted replied, “I don’t believe that Jesus is God.” “Well,” I said, “then you can read my paper.” That was the beginning of a loving friendship that has continued through correspondence after Ted moved to Yuma, Arizona, to be with his son Chuck and daughter-in-law, Bonnie. (Ted and I both start with Jesus the man whose total embodiment of God’s wisdom and God’s love and his complete obedience to God’s will lead to us calling him also divine. Many Christians start with his divinity and sometimes do not pay much attention to his humanity.)

I enjoy serving on the Resident Council and am on my second term at present. I also volunteer in the office once a week, play bridge, work jigsaw puzzles and have many friends.

My love of opera has been enabled by my friendship with Barbara Anne Keely, associate professor of Christian education and spiritual formation at United. We are regulars at the Minnesota Opera productions and a few years ago we traveled to Seattle for the full Ring cycle by Wagner. The Metropolitan Opera offered the Ring in St. Paul when I was ten years old. My father took me to Walkyrie and Siegfried and I was permanently hooked.

In 2003, I was approached to lead the first ever capital campaign of People, Incorporated. Sydney Lange, long-time chair of the Board, remembered me from the Junior League and our work together on the Community Chest campaign.

Five Presbyterian ministers had created this agency in the late ‘60s to deal with the mental health issues returning Vietnam veterans were facing.
