It had remained a small necessary effort until Dr. Tim Burkett and his new administrative assistant, Barbara Nichols, took over in the early ‘90s. Tim had experienced mental illness in his family. Barbara had the temperament to take on and enjoy finding new financial resources. Together, with an enlarged staff, they created an agency dedicated to creating new ways to help those with persistent and severe mental illness, many also homeless.

Collaboration with Dr. Charles Schultz and the Department of Psychiatry at the University was a key element of the $5.4-million campaign. This was a cutting-edge dream that was a delight to talk about in the community. With a fine committee we raised $5.6 million. And I was honored in 2005 as Minnesota’s Volunteer Fund Raiser of the year.

[The following account of the Ugandan children turned out to be fraud.
Grandson Dan Crawley, in law school at the University, gathered the evidence from the web and presented it in a way that allowed Nana to reach her own conclusions.
She understood quickly what had happened and quickly moved to terminate the arrangement.]

Early in 2006 I received an unexpected letter forwarded from the Manitou Island address. It was from a young woman from Kampala, Uganda, East Africa, whose parents had died of HIV/AIDS. She had four younger siblings and had herself been accepted at the University. Her priest had my name from theology books that I had sent to Africa before I moved. I sent her a small check in return and told her of my desire to help.

She had enrolled her siblings, Sarah, Mary and twins, John and Jane, in a school and she sent me the headmaster’s e-mail address. Mukiibi Nasser began to take an interest in these children, and I developed a confidence in his suggestions. All funds are now transferred from my bank to his, which is in New York.

In a year’s time property has been bought where crops can be raised. On it a house has been built (3 bedrooms, electricity, plumbing, etc. for the absurdly low price of $12,000). Christine is at the top of her University class. The others are nearly so.

It turned out that John was born with a hole in his heart. When I worried about financing an operation, Mukiibi found someone to pay the cost in Madras, India if I would pay the airfare for John and a nurse. He is now fully recovered.

When I asked Mukiibi what I should give the children of Christmas, his response was, “To be precise, they have nothing, no toothpaste, no soap, no hygienic supplies. A monthly stipend would be the most help.”

And so the stipend is in place, and I have guaranteed in my will their educations through the University. Christine asked if they should call me mother or grandmother. I opted for the latter, so I am now Jja jja Molly. And there is a final lovely coincidence. Christine and I have the same birth date. Although I do not ever expect to meet these grandchildren, our relationship continues to deepen. Christine and I exchange e-mails. The younger children have written lovely notes.

title: Poetic Odds and Ends
summary: Molly‘s computer had a lot of writing on it — sermons, poetry, beginnings of documents, sketches of times and places. Some of these she expanded and incorporated into her memoir. “Poetic Odds and Ends” was the final part of her memoir, a mosaic of verses she evidently found pleasing or important but couldn’t find a place for.

title: My Father
summary: C.H. Bigelow Jr., Molly’s father, was a widower with three children when he married Molly’s mother, Allison McKibbin. C.H. was 53 when Molly was born; newborn Molly had three young-adult siblings aged 18, 21, and 24.
content:
*Ich liebe dich mit ganzen herzen.* My father said that to me one day as we were coming in the back vestibule of 530 Grand Avenue. Did he mean me or was it just a lesson in German pronunciation? My German professor at college once asked me how it was that I spoke German with such a good accent — ch’s at the back of the throat, not k’s or sh’s. I told her that my father had been a singer and had taught me

But I like to think that he was saying he loved me. He was 53 when I was born, so that in one sense I was his first grandchild, making for a special bond. I have a picture of us taken when I was about a year old. We are laughing at each other as I am reaching for his pocket watch that he is holding up. We are laughing *with* each other, as do those who love each other.

My father’s voice had mostly gone before I knew him, due to the bronchial catarrh that has plagued many of us, but it was evidently very fine. He could play the accompaniments of the Schubert songs that were his favorites. He had played the flute at Williams College. I remember that flute, but don’t know what happened to it.

Devoted to House of Hope Presbyterian Church, he was an elder — for life in those days — and was chair of the Music Committee. When the building on Summit was erected, his primary interest was the installation of the organ. He was a man of deep but undisclosed faith, a man of wisdom to be called on at difficult times.

He was also a civic leader, serving as president of the St. Paul Chamber of Commerce and, among other things, sparking the completion of the Athletic Club building which had stood unfinished during World War I. Later on he would become, as chair of the Board of Trustees, the guiding force for Macalester College during the Depression years.

Here I need to make clear who he was. Identified always by his initials, he was C.H. Bigelow Jr. until 1911 when his father died. At that time, since there were no Social Security identifications in that era, he dropped the Junior and my half-brother, Charlie, who had been the IIIrd, became Junior.

He was *not* H.H. Bigelow of Brown and Bigelow. The business community gave a luncheon for H.H. on his return from Leavenworth prison after having served a time for tax evasion. H.H. arrived in his prison suit, and having been asked to give a few remarks said, in effect, you would have all done the same if you had been smart enough. That, as they say, was that.

Our family model was that first Charles Henry, who, as secretary of St. Paul Fire and Marine Insurance Company, went to Chicago after the Great Fire and promised that all insurance claims would be paid off 100 cents on the dollar, even though he had to raise the necessary funds when he returned to St Paul. In 1906, as president, he made the same promise to the citizens of San Francisco after their disastrous earthquake. We never took kindly to the notion that we were in some way connected to Brown and Bigelow.

My father was a gentle man. Third in his family between George, who got into trouble through gambling, womanizing and eventually embezzling, and Frederic (F.R.), who was of a flamboyant nature, but also a leader in St. Paul. Fred followed his father as president of Fire and Marine. The eldest in the family was Emma, and father often told me how she had written in the barn on College Avenue “Do goob, be goob.” Albert, who in adulthood lived in Louisville, and Deane, who died in his tenth year, completed the family. I have Deane’s Florentine cup, given to me for my first Christmas by my grandmother.

After graduation from Williams in 1887, C.H. spent a summer in Europe. His diaries show a change in penmanship overnight, but not why. He spent a year or more in New York City working, I suppose, in the insurance world. During that time he tried to deal with George’s peccadilloes, but was too gentle. His father sent Fred to handle the matter. George was given a ticket to Hawaii. And my grandmother was not told of any of this until the outcome was final. Thanks be that we have moved away from such father-knows-best strategies.

Father came back to St. Paul and worked his way up from counting nuts and bolts to the presidency of Farwell, Ozmun, Kirk and Co. His leadership was paternal, setting all wages and salaries himself, but the company was one of two wholesale hardware companies in Minnesota to survive the Depression and the advent of Montgomery Ward catalogs. His own salary was very modest, $6,000 per year at the time of his death in 1943. Other income derived from large holdings of St. Paul Fire and Marine stock.

On January 30, 1896, he had married Florence Fairchild. Florence was musical, an early member of the Schubert Club. She was also imaginative, designing the note-laden logo for the club that is still in use today. She was the middle daughter of the Henry Fairchilds who lived at the east end of Sunfish Lake. My father would ride his bicycle out to their home to do his courting.

Their first child, Donald, was born November 10, 1896. Alida was born March 25, 1898, and Charlie on March 26, 1901, but by then, or soon after, Florence became ill with tuberculosis and died October 21, 1905. A year or so had been spent in the bracing climate of Colorado without result. My father kept a picture of Florence in a locket on his watch fob. Occasionally throughout his life he would bring it out to gaze at it quietly.

Father and my mother, Allison McKibbin, 15 years younger, became acquainted when they both served on the building committee for the YWCA, which was to be built on Fifth Street just west of Washington. They were married on August 26, 1911, and after a honeymoon climbing Mt. Snowden in Wales where he would stride on ahead of her and then move on as soon as she caught up, they returned to the fully functioning household at 415 Laurel Avenue in winter and Manitou Island in White Bear Lake, fifteen miles northeast of St. Paul, in the summer. Miss Nimms, the house and child keeper for six years, moved out to become Mrs. Beddie and Mother took over. She never spoke much about those years, saying only that they had hurried adoption proceedings because Donald was almost fifteen. Those years were also filled with miscarriages so that they had started proceedings for adoption when I held on through a difficult birth on July 30, 1919. My brother John came along easily on December 27, 1920.

Rather than having neighborhood friendships that might have developed more easily if we had lived at 415 in the summer, I guess I was more comfortable visiting my maternal grandparents who lived on Virginia Street, a short distance away. One time Grampie took me with him to W.A. Frost’s drugstore. He bought me a glass jar of Whitman’s sour balls. I promptly dropped it on the granite floor. Without a word he bought me another. It was an early example of unmerited forgiveness.

A few years ago I had the opportunity to tour 415 Laurel, which had been saved from the wrecking ball by regentrification. It was just as I had remembered it, except that the rooms all seemed much, much smaller.

In the fall of 1923, we moved from Laurel Avenue to 530 Grand Avenue. This large vine-covered home with Gothic windows stood at the top of the Grand Avenue Hill. Its floor plan continued the ecclesiastical theme, dining room and parlor as transepts, hall and living room as nave, vestibule and front porch as chancel. There was also the convenience of a tunnel to the three-car garage below it on Heather Place.

Father’s pocket watch continued to be an important part of my early life. It was mine to hold and to learn from during the long sermons when, after Sunday school, we went to church. It was marked with 5, 10, 15’s in addition to the usual 1, 2, 3’s and so I learned to count by fives. It also answered my unspoken question, How much longer? about Dr. Swearingen, who, with arms held wide, round face topped with a mass of white hair, was preaching with a powerful voice; so powerful that my mind places him in the high pulpit, not built for another fifty years.

I wonder if it is because of that pocket watch that I visualize numbers to this day that go in a circle around to 12, then to the right to 20, down to 60, left to 100 and straight up again to 12. My 60th birthday was depressing. It took me six months to get over that milestone. The next turn is 100 and I hope I may live that long.

Summers were always spent on Manitou Island, surrounded by uncles, aunts and cousins. Father’s exercise was rowing. One time he rowed with me — I was too little to help — all the way to Wildwood, where we stopped in a little park to the east of the amusement complex and he read to me from the Brownie Book. And of course he rowed back, close to five miles round trip.

His other love was the woods across the road from our house that he also owned. Almost every night he would take his sickle and trim the paths that he had made, and we would walk along them, the first one in line breaking the spider webs. After Father’s death, Mother, with great regret, sold the woods. Three homes now stand in it.

Father loved automobiles, the faster the better. One time at County Road C and Highway 61 a policeman stopped him for going 35 miles an hour. I was lying down in the back seat at the time. When I reached twelve, my father decided to pass on his skills. My only memory of that process was of stalling the car in front of a streetcar at Seven Corners, and my father’s overly patient encouragement for me to get the car started again.

Driving with a clutch in those days was an interesting experience when you lived on the top of a hill. I can remember many years later when it was imperative not to stop at all during slippery winter weather if you wished to get home from downtown. Licenses, I believe, were issued at age 15, but were simply on request. It was a simpler time, but I still drive a bit too fast. Cruise controls are a great invention.

Many years after Father’s death I would get an occasional phone call from George Marzolf, “Molly, I’ve been thinking about your father.” And so we would chat for a while. George Marzolf was the head of the sheet metal shop housed in the FOK warehouse on the west side. All the things that George worked on were dear to my father’s heart, and we visited the shop often. George invented the machine that turned out Cheerios and so we could substitute them for soggy corn flakes that were our standard fare on Sunday nights. George invented the first sheet metal draw that made sinks out of one piece of stainless steel. We had that first sink in our house on the Island. When Dick and I remodeled many years later I should have sent it to the Historical Society. Mea culpa. But George’s sheet metal work lives on in our family because my son Douglas and his wife, Ann Harris, bought George’s summer cottage on the Wisconsin side of the St. Croix River a few miles south of Hudson. Stainless steel fills their kitchen.

There are more vignettes: riding horseback with him at Paradise Ranch in Wyoming, learning double entry bookkeeping by keeping his books — all loving times. Following my wedding on June 26, 1943, he had a heart attack after chasing after my car that was rolling down the hill. He could not recover and I was called home from Miami early in October. He said, Molly, but lapsed into a coma soon after. The moment of his death on October 5, 1943, remains vivid, because, looking away, I saw a “gathering of energy” move through the air and away from the foot of the bed. Looking back at him, I saw he was no longer there. Only the body was left.

Returning to Florida after 10 days or so, I discovered that I was pregnant. Having a baby seemed to me to be an extension of my father’s life. It was almost as if his being was entering into me now, an unbroken continuation. On another front, it was the affirmation that our marriage was going to work. I had so much to learn about Dick, about men who were different from my father. I had so much to learn about living together and sharing each other’s lives, that this new life within me would mean a new life altogether.

title: The Woods
summary: Molly was a perceptive and versatile writer. Her memoir includes several original poems and a collection of poetic odds and ends. “The Woods” celebrates her father’s “other great love.”
content: Stuff

title: My Mother
summary: Although she did not feel close to her mother, Molly did learn learn some important things from her: how to write, that injustice must be confronted forcefully ... and how to knit.
content:
My Mother and Father met officially as members of a building committee for the YWCA that was to be located on Fifth Street where The Travelers Insurance Company, successor to The St. Paul Fire and Marine Insurance Company, now stands. He was fifteen years older than she, a widower with three young children. When they married in 1911, Donald was just under 15, Alida, 13 and Charlie, 10. She never spoke much about those early years. It must have been difficult to become their mother. Yet in later years, for the two younger at least, she was very much their Mother.

My birth was long awaited. It came eight years later and only after adoption papers had been filed. It was also exceedingly difficult, some two weeks or so late, a long labor and breech. Small wonder that my mother could look with special favor on my brother John who came easily a year and a half later. Nor was I an easy child to love. Do difficult births mark babies in some way? I wonder.

Mrs. Williams, who had cared for me after my birth as was the custom, returned the next summer. I had been weaned to the cup after eight months and in June my Mother, newly pregnant, my Father, Alida and Charlie, went to Europe for the summer. Donald, following service in World War I, had entered the diplomatic corps and was stationed in Paris. What an opportune time to visit, especially for Mother who, before her marriage, had spent several summers in England and in Italy!

Over the years Mother impressed upon me that Mrs. Williams had taken very good care of me. She impressed it upon me so thoroughly that it was only fairly recently that I began to wonder what my own reactions might have been. A one-year-old might possibly have felt abandoned when her whole immediate family disappeared. Did it perhaps intensify my feelings of rejection when John and my boy cousins would later run away from me, quite normally, in play?

What is real and what we feel can be so different. There was plenty of family nearby on the Island that summer: my grandmother Bigelow, Grampie and Grammie McKibbin, two sets of aunts and uncles and cousins of my own age. I was cared for (not only by Mrs. Williams), but my own family was missing. Could they really love me if they had abandoned me?

It is difficult for me to write about my Mother. We were not close. I was the one when we were young who could never answer when she would say, “Quickly, tell me three things that are beautiful.” John, of course, was johnny-on-the-spot. My unreasoned feelings of alienation from her led to my rejecting much of what she immersed herself in, the worlds of poetry and art. I can still feel tired when I remember the trips we made to the Minneapolis Art Institute. She wanted me to memorize many passages from the Bible as she had done as a child. I was not able to and still cannot. As an adult, I was relieved to discover from conversation with a psychologist that her desire was probably the cause. Later on, I was also relieved to discover from my brother, whom I had assumed understood everything Mother might discuss, that he did not always understand her either.

Perhaps here too is the reason that during my college years I was afraid to think, to expose myself to any of the courses that would have expanded my mind, and would have allowed us ever really to converse. Or perhaps it was simply that once having felt unloved, however untrue, criticism — no matter how constructive — would feel like an attack.

It was in the world of ideas that my Mother lived. When I visualize her, she is sitting at her desk. Other daughters may have learned to sew or cook from their mothers. I learned to write letters, to use the vertical pronoun as little as possible. She did, however, teach me to knit, a talent I have valued for over seventy years.

Mother did not go to college, but was self-taught, ranging widely in many areas. Intrigued by Arnold Toynbee’s theories of history, she once drove all the way to Princeton, New Jersey, to hear him lecture. She also was interested in the predictions of Edgar Cayce, a famous psychic and predictor of the future. One prediction that I remember was that California was going to fall into the ocean. Do we need to revisit Cayce? This interest stemmed, I believe, from her deep conviction — one that I share — that “the other side” is very close. What gave her strength to raise the older children was her belief that Florence, their mother, was nearby.

Mother was a forceful woman willing to take on all injustice. Once at the Art Institute a guard picked up a youngster by the collar and gave him such a kick that he flew through the air and landed at the foot of the stone steps. We immediately marched up to the manager’s office to complain. I am now sorry to say that I cringed with embarrassment.

But I am not sorry that I learned that racial discrimination was unacceptable, not Christian. The n-word was never used in our home. Mother worked with the Rev. and Mrs. Camp of Zion Presbyterian Church on their Welcome Hall, a community resource for black girls, and was outraged in the 1930s when a sexual misstep by Mr. Camp was used as an excuse to close the church. Only Dayton Avenue Presbyterian Church, after some persuading, was willing to take Zion’s members.

Mother was also a forceful decision maker, many times for others. Occasionally this was a bit too much for family members, but there were other families in the community who were helped by her willingness to run interference with bureaucratic structures. Family Service, at the time, had a program of volunteer mentors. As we were growing up there were many stories at the dinner table about “little Matilda,” a single mother with three young daughters. Mother’s final coup for that family was to talk my father into giving jobs to all three of the daughters’ husbands.

Since Mother, when she married, had become the mistress of a full staff of helpers, she never learned to cook or iron. After my Father died and she came to Florida to help us with our first child, I, self-taught, instructed her in both. But she was inventive. Since we had no washing machine, she decided that the heavy dew at night and the hot sun by day were sufficient to make clean the sheets. It is interesting to me, as I think about this, that I have absolutely no memory of how I washed the sheets in the year before she came and the several months after she left. I presume it was in the sink. I then carried them dripping to the clothesline.

We were under war-time rationing and Mother was naturally frugal. Nevertheless on my first night home from the hospital following Ricky’s birth, my Naval Air Corps instructor husband and I were somewhat amazed to be served Kraft macaroni and cheese complete with cooked black bugs. She had called the Kraft salesman to complain but decided that the dinner should not be wasted. TWO Kraft dinners appeared at our door the next day courtesy of an apologetic salesman. When Mother tackled a problem she did not cease and desist until it was solved.

Mother was a writer. Privately published, *The Bethlehem of Opa-Locka* was written during her stay with us. Earlier, at Paradise Ranch, she had compiled many of John’s and my early conversations in *Private Joy*.

My Mother’s name was Allison. Spelled with two l’s, it is a family surname. She used to tell us that when she was little, she took a dislike to the name and thought that she would like to change it, imagining something more romantic such as Rosamund. When she approached her father on the subject, he did not even put aside the newspaper, simply saying, “Very well, we will call you Lizzie.” It is my Mother who has made her name more popular. Three of her granddaughters as well as a number of other little girls throughout St. Paul have been named Allison.

Mother was one of the first two women trustees of Macalester College. On her retirement she was awarded an honorary LL.D. It was a thrilling moment for her. It was a fitting tribute to an exceptional woman. In her last years she suffered from Parkinson’s disease and was bedridden. She died on July 7, 1970. September 14 would have been her 89th birthday.

title: Childhood
summary: Summers found Molly on Manitou Island, a Bigelow retreat that was “a summer playground for for its many children in the 1920s and ’30s.” Her children and grandchildren knew exactly what she meant.
content:
When we moved to 530 Grand Avenue I very quickly found Hope and Jean Horner whose large back yard backed up to ours. They had a swing set and they had toys in their playroom. Both already went to Oak Hall, a private school for girls. When it became time for me to go to Kindergarten, I suggested to Daddy that I would like to go there also. His reply was a firm No. I was to go to the Summit School which my Aunt Alice and Uncle Fred Bigelow had been instrumental in starting.

One Sunday afternoon I declined to go with my father and mother and John on a trip in the automobile, preferring to play with the Horners. When my family returned they told me that they had gone up in an airplane. I would not have that experience until my junior year in college.

Connie Shepard was another friend, living close by on the corner of Summit and Lawton. Connie had a governess who introduced me to peanut butter. Occasionally I would be invited to eat dinner with the family including Connie’s two older brothers, Roger and Blake. The tomato soup was served with a dab of sour cream on it. Connie also went to Summit until she went east to boarding school. One morning Connie called me to come over right away. The Shepards were Christian Scientists and Connie’s baby brother Stanley had just been born at home.

Although we did not have dabs of sour cream on our soup, our food was always interesting, depending in part on the nationality of the one who was ruling in the kitchen, pork roast with plums or fruit soup for example.

As soon as school was out we moved out to Manitou Island. Invariably, no matter what the date, it always seemed that we would have two weeks of cold and rain. Such is the impatience of youth.

In the 1920s and ’30s the island was a summer playground for its many children. Tennis, swimming and boating, or reading and cards on rainy days, made up the long lazy days. For variety we might bike to White Bear — we called it the Village — for a root beer shake (not float) at the White Bear Drugstore on Washington Park. For excitement we could be taken to Wildwood, the amusement park at the south end of the lake, which had a roller coaster, ferris wheel, bumper cars, and a tilt-awhirl.

My parents, older than the rest of the parents, did not swim so I was taken to a class at the White Bear Yacht Club when I was about seven. There I was told to jump off the dock, and I can still remember the feel of the instructor’s toe as he kept me up until I learned to dogpaddle. I never did learn the proper strokes and had to take swimming to graduate from college, but my confidence in the water was boundless.

On the lake there were no motorboats. We either rowed or sailed. Taught to stay with the boat if it should capsize, we wore no life jackets. To my knowledge there were none to be worn. Many times we might take a rowboat to the middle of the lake, and then drift and talk and sometimes swim.

My brother John, at about the age of ten, was one of the first four proud owners of the Johnson boat works new X-boat. It was declared safe and almost tipless. Sailing became his special love, racing three times a week. Later he graduated to a C-boat, a scow which only skillful handling could keep from tipping in a strong wind. John surely inherited the McKibbin sailing genes.

Although there were tennis courts on the Island, I did not have lessons and only occasionally played when in a group. My partner had to remind me to keep my eye on the ball. During the very hot drought years I would watch with some amazement at players walking by, much preferring to read with improvised air conditioning consisting of a fan blowing across a block of ice.

The Lightners next door had a playhouse. We must have played in it but what I remember is jumping off the roof! And then tragedy struck. Caroline was taken ill, an illness that resulted in the paralysis of her legs. Caroline took this affliction with great grace, and so her friends shared many hours of cards, of listening to the radio and learning the lyrics of the popular songs. Alice, Caroline and Mary went to Oak Hall so that we did not see each other in the wintertime.

Nor should I forget the amount of time spent up in our climbing tree. We had several seats on its branches. That lovely maple eventually succumbed to the number of nails in it.

Dancing school was another childhood activity. We started when Summit School girls were in fifth grade and St. Paul Academy boys were in the sixth grade. Mrs. Higby with bright red hair and a long black lace dress was our instructor. Dancing school lasted for three years as I remember. I was not popular.

title: Education
summary: Molly was a pioneer, the only physics major in the Vassar Class of 1941. She understood the physics of what she could not see — atoms, waves, particles — but the mathematics that described observable physical forces, not so much. Music, however, was a lifelong joy.
content:
I attended Summit School, a college preparatory school for girls, for thirteen years. It provided wonderful training academically and also for future leadership as women. I ended up as treasurer of the Students’ Association and then as president during one semester of my senior year. I was a good student, but as I have suggested in writing about my mother, something kept me from really learning to think creatively for myself. And as a social butterfly I was a dud. The only time the boys stood in line to dance with me at dancing school was when a group of us in sixth grade decided to play hooky. One of the mothers — mine — found us going into the Paramount (now the Park) Theater. Male admiration was brief.

This creative thinking problem carried on with me to Vassar College. At that particular moment, Vassar had decided to dispense with freshman requirements. As a result I took neither history nor English in college, now to my great regret. I took the “safe” subjects that for me were math and science, and I ended up majoring in physics. This turned out to be a curious major. I was not good at the thought problems presented in either the fields of math or physics. And I was the only major in my class.

The summer of my junior year I spent six weeks working with a group from Wilson College at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge. Our job was to record the positions of wave patterns formed by electromagnetic radiation from a spectroscope. It was the sort of technical work that I was good at.

Comprehensive exam time came at the end of my senior year. What I understood about physics was what I could not see, atoms and molecules and the like. What I could not manage were the formulas required for a ball rolling down an inclined plane. I was told that I had not passed the exam, but that I would still graduate. The honor of the department was evidently at stake. In my junior year I had been awarded a prize that alternated between the Astronomy and Physics departments. I had protested this to President McCracken, but he had merely shrugged his shoulders and told me that the publicity had already been sent out. I had put myself in a box that I could not get out of. The department deserved my grateful thanks because I knew that at whatever time I might take that comprehensive over again I would never be able to pass it.

I continue to be interested in that unseen portion of the physical realm, reading about how atoms act as waves or particles depending on the interest of the observer. Chaos theory has opened up new vistas also.

I took singing lessons all four Vassar years, but not for credit. What a difference that might have made in my life if I been able to use those lessons as part of a major in music! Music 101 had turned out to be unsatisfactory because I did not have the gift of gab, the flowery language needed to express aesthetics. I had also had piano lessons for many years, but did not stick to the practice needed to become proficient.

But I was very musical. Mother’s little book, *Private Joy*, says that my dancing was something to watch as a little child. I could march in rhythm and sing and play the piano at an early age. Caruso records at my McKibbin grandparents are vivid early childhood memories. I would march around their home dragging a pull toy behind me. I had joined Mr. Morton’s little girl’s choir at age eight with joy. Singing released something in me. Through it I found a playfulness and freedom that didn’t seem to be in other parts of my world.

Singing in Vassar’s choir was a great experience. We practiced *a cappella*, and Mr. Geer, with perfect pitch, would announce, “Altos, you are two vibrations low.” It was amazing how we could begin to sense that ourselves. We sang Christmas carols from the tower of the library. We sang once in Carnegie Hall and gave one memorable concert with the Harvard Glee Club of Kodaly’s *Te Deum*.

Vassar was also the place where I discovered East Coast racial prejudice of a character unknown to me in St. Paul. There were surprising, to me, conversations about the decision to admit the first black student. The closely knit group on the third floor of Jewett was not of one voice in that discussion. Nor was it united when I innocently invited a Jewish transfer student living next door to join our group that would be drawing for rooms in Main to which we all moved for our senior year. Fortunately she and I were able to join a group less influenced by parental advice, and I began an enduring friendship with my new roommate, Harriet (Boo) Gibbs (Gardiner) of Jacksonville, Florida.

Enduring friendships have also continued with members of that original group; in particular, Lois Glover (Creighton), my roommate the first two years, and with Mary Dykema (Maguire) who lived next door. Lois and I both ended up becoming Presbyterian ministers.

Over thirty years later I went back to school, complete with blue exam books, at United Theological Seminary of the Twin Cities. In the interim my education continued in the Junior League.

title: Marriage and Children
summary: Dick and Molly were two very different people, Molly wrote, but they shared a great range of interests, including a healthy dose of international golfing.
content:
On graduation from Vassar College in 1941 the first thing I did on returning home was to join the House of Hope choir. In those days the Motet Choir was small with 14 members, but there was an auxiliary choir, unpaid, called the Westminster Choir. We practiced with the Motet Choir so it was almost the same as today’s single group. I was sick for a couple of weeks in the fall and when I returned there was Dick (Richard) McMillan sitting in the baritone section. His mother, Elsa, the contralto soloist, had told him to stop singing the *Hallelujah Chorus* in the shower and to come to practice.

Dick’s father, Malcolm Dana McMillan, was an accomplished organist and was the founder and director of the Orpheus Club, a male chorus. He had died in 1939 from complications following an appendix operation, partly because he had had polio at the age of 21. I remembered him at an Orpheus Club concert coming on stage with the help of two canes.

Dick and I had been in Sunday school together. We both remembered getting our Bibles from Mrs. Beaver Wade Day in the primary department, but in those days the classes were segregated, girls and boys. In the junior department, he was absent since he was a boy soprano over at St. John the Evangelist Episcopal Church. Before he got back I had left Sunday school because dear Mrs. Redpath was a believer in the inerrancy of the Bible. In those days you did not often venture to disagree with your elders. My parents, and therefore I, had a different view of the Biblical witness, and so after discussion I went only to church.

Since I was the tallest of the women in the Westminster Choir it wasn’t long before Dick and I were sitting together. I tell you this because it always amazed me. He came to know me in my most freedom-filled moments.

Our first date followed singing in a big Christmas pageant put on downtown. Then followed attempts at skiing. Brother John told me that if I fell down I was to come up smiling. Visits to the Chi Psi fraternity followed, Dick obviously disappointed that I did not own the requisite little black dress.

My friend, Josephine Bowlin, wanted me to join her in singing with the Bach Society of the University and Dick came along. Although he never learned to read music, he had an excellent ear. That spring we sang the *B Minor Mass* and the *Magnificat*. What a wonderful spring we had together.

In May, 1942 Dick enlisted in the Navy, we became engaged, and were married a year later after he had received his wings as a naval aviator. In the meantime, I spent a year in the Motet Choir, received my first paycheck ($2/week), learned how to be a Red Cross nurses’ aide, and had joined the Junior League, which required community volunteer hours.

We were married June 26, 1943, on three days notice after Dick completed eight flawless carrier deck landings out of the Great Lakes Naval Air Station. The ceremony was held at 530 Grand Avenue, Dr. Edwin Kagin officiating. Allison Butler, my sister Alida’s daughter, was my maid of honor. Dick’s older brother, Malcolm, was best man. All the furniture was moved to the basement, a window from the parlor was taken out, and a ramp added so that the reception could be held in the backyard. Such was the ingenuity of my parents on that 95-degree day.

After a brief fishing trip (my first and last) to Gull Lake, we drove to Miami, Florida, and to our home for almost two years: 1156 Sinbad Avenue, Opa Locka, Florida, one of a group of identical, 30-by-36-foot Navy houses. Dick had orders as an instructor in dive bombing. With gas rationing, we did not venture far, but made friends with the other instructors, and the base was close by with its commissary and movies.

Richard Jr. was born the following June 16th, my mother coming to help. And help she did because Dick became ill with viral pneumonia and spent a month in the hospital. She visited him daily. I was not allowed until he was well into recovery.

In the fall the dive bomber instructors were sent to the fleet, but Dick, who was always a superb “people person,” was held over and became an instrument instructor. It was not until April of 1945 that he received his orders to report to California for training as a fighter pilot. He had refused a suggestion that he go as a night fighter. Since I was pregnant and having some problems, we thought it best that I go home to Minnesota. V-J day arrived before Dick could join the fleet.

Charles Bigelow was born October 9, 1945. We were living at 530 Grand at the time. In the fall of 1946 we moved to the White Bear Lake house, which my mother winterized for us, but in the meantime, Dick and his brother Malcolm started McMillan Manufacturing Company in the garage at 530. Mal’s father-in-law designed an electric motor and the three went into business. After a number of months, Mr. Schmidt dropped out and the two brothers moved their enterprise to a warehouse in downtown St. Paul. I have written [the story of the motor business](https://www.frauerica.org/Text/McMillanMfg.php) in another place.

The three years on Manitou Island were a happy time, but when Ricky was ready to start kindergarten we found 1917 Bayard Avenue in the Highland Park area of St. Paul. It was to be our home for the next 25 years. Horace Mann, the elementary school, was nearby and playmates were even closer. Dick took up golf at Highland Park and would take the two boys with him, while I became more active in Junior League work. Babysitters were cheap.

As a baby, Ricky had the habit of rocking on his knees in his crib and then putting down his head to see if he was ready for sleep. It was quite comical to watch. As he got older, he also was happy to stand and watch others. For some reason this bothered his teacher in kindergarten who had him tested for intelligence. The results must have made the teacher feel a little foolish. Rick was also very musical. While in elementary school he played Bach preludes with considerable understanding and feeling. He gave up the piano on his move to the St. Paul Academy, but his native ability enabled him to pick up tunes off the radio on his guitar.

Charley was a happy friendly child who had two serious bouts of illness. One was a strangulated bowel requiring emergency surgery, the other an attack of rheumatic fever. The last kept him in bed for the summer and prevented him from playing on the Little League team that he had been asked to join. Penicillin on a daily basis kept him from having a recurrence of the illness. And we thanked God for his recovery from a near fatal automobile accident while he was in college. Dick and I spent two weeks in Springfield, Massachusetts, until he became lucid and did not bring him home for several weeks after that.

After I had two spontaneous abortions, (miscarriages) Douglas deHaas was born prematurely on July 19, 1950. He weighed 4 lbs. 13 oz and had to stay in the hospital until he had gained another pound. Sadly, he had to go back to the hospital with pneumonia in October. I had hoped to nurse him for a longer time. He prospered, however, and became our child known for imaginative problem solving. It is he who found a springerle board for me for Christmas so that I could make the beloved cookies that his grandmother McMillan always served. As a child he did not seem to me to have an ear for music so he did not have piano lessons. That was an unfortunate decision because he also has a fine voice. And he is the one who worked most closely with Dick and has now the ownership of McMillan Electric Company. And many of the clothes I wear are thanks to his continuing imagination having to do with style.

Mary Allison was born on April 21, 1952. We now had two pairs. Allison looked so much like her brothers that when I first saw her I was tempted to unwrap her and see for myself what they were telling me! Alli was so quietly competent in everything that she did that I remember once realizing that she was, therefore, one easy to neglect. And I regret that I did. Allison was a tennis player, winning many tournaments, and I did not attend a single one of them. She also wanted to be a figure skater. I could not imagine myself as a figure skater’s mother. When she joined the first women’s ice hockey team in the United States at Brown University, I did go and watch her in her first tournament in Montreal.

We thought we were finished, but to our surprise and ultimate delight, Anne Elizabeth arrived on June 29, 1956. Black hair instead of brown, she had a whole different look about her. It was she who eventually discovered that her nose was small like Uncle Mal’s, instead of the rest of our more sturdy versions. She also could sing, coloratura style, at a very early age. Therefore the ear infections to which she was prone took on great importance, and were successfully treated. Sing she did, winning a Schubert Club award at age 17.

Family dinners were a nightly occurrence. So was weekly Sunday school and church. Rick was ten years old before Dick and I took our first vacation away from them — and heard about misdeeds on the part of both children and babysitter when we returned!

A new era in our lives began when Dick, who was president of McMillan Manufacturing Company before he was forty years old, was invited to join the Young Presidents Organization. Through YPO and its regular meetings we developed many friendships in the Twin Cities and nationally. Twin Town Investors, though not limited to the wives of YPO members, was another outgrowth of this relationship. YPOers graduated at fifty, and so it was with great excitement that we convened a year or two later as the World Business Council. WBC held its first meeting at Innisbrook Resort in Tarpon Springs, Florida, that had, among its many beautiful appointments, three golf courses. Before we left we had bought an apartment. The building called Portrush was to be our home away from home for the next twenty years.

About this time, Mal and Dick decided it was time to sell McMillan Manufacturing Company. Following the worship service one morning at House of Hope Dick casually mentioned to the pastor, Calvin Didier, that he wished to give $200,000 to the church. I was as surprised as Cal. The amount was approximately a tithe of 10 percent of Dick’s portion of the sale. At Mr. Didier’s suggestion, this became the Elsa and Malcolm McMillan Ministry of Music Endowment.

Under WBC auspices we took two longer trips, one to Argentina, filled with the lively antics of penguins, and one to China in 1978. We were one of the early western groups to enter China and were greeted with great curiosity. Both men and women wore Mao suits and their transportation was by bicycle. My first encounter with a restroom was memorable as I tried, with camera and purse around my neck, to manage just a hole in the floor. In addition to the usual sights, we visited a commune of 250,000 people, with apartments of two or three rooms holding three generations.

Gilbert and Sullivan’s *H.M.S. Pinafore* was scheduled as the spring play at St. Paul Academy when Rick was a senior because he and Charley and Dana, their cousin at Summit School, all had fine singing voices. The performances were memorable and were the basis for a special version that the children produced for our 40th wedding anniversary. We had just been invited for dinner at Doug’s and there were all the children and grandchildren! What a loving moment!

Our children know how blessed they were to have their father. He had grown up with children living next door who loved him. He was always ready for some play. He would have been happy to have a dozen of his own. It was his parenting that grounded the family.

We were so very different. A little book by Lois Wyse, *Love Songs for the Very Married,* became important for us. Dick kept it in his bedside table drawer. The title of another poem by Wyse states our differences most bluntly: “Just Because I Share Your Name Does Not Mean I Share Your Opinion.” That worked both ways! But neither of us was able verbally to share our emotions easily.

With the children grown, we were able to spend many happy winters at Innisbrook. As apartment owners we belonged to the golf club with many scheduled activities. It was, and is, a beautiful place with lakes, alligators, ibis, wood storks, and nightly “ball hawking” to retrieve the errant balls of visitors.

Golfing vacations also meant that we played many famous courses: St. Andrews, Muirfield, Carnoustie, Turnberry, Gleneagles, Troon in Scotland, Ballybunion, Royal County Down in Ireland. And Gullane #1 is memorable because twosomes had right of way. As Scotsmen sat on the tee I prayed for good drives!

Having spent several idyllic vacations as a child and as a young adult at dude (guest) ranches in Wyoming, I suggested that our fiftieth wedding anniversary in June 1993 might be celebrated in the same manner. Allison took over and discovered Vista Verde, a small ranch in Steamboat Springs, Colorado. The owners of this small ranch decided that McMillans would be the only visitors that week. What a grand time they gave us! The picture of us with our five children, their five spouses and our fifteen grandchildren is one that I treasure.

The previous year Warren Bjorklund had invited Dick to a golf tournament in Carefree, Arizona. The spring of ‘94 we found ourselves selling our Innisbrook apartment and buying a house in Carefree on the corner of Stagecoach Drive and Peaceful Place. The house was nestled up against Black Mountain and was equipped with a pool and hot tub. It also had a motor home garage that we remodeled into a guesthouse with the help of John Austin who had helped with some redecorating on Manitou Island. We spent an especially happy year there. But Dick’s diabetes was beginning to act up. He had had a heart attack in Florida, and a trip to the Mayo Clinic in Scottsdale told us that there was oncoming diabetic senility. He decided not to join Desert Forest Golf Club, and unaccountably he began to talk about selling the house.

Dick died on June 15, 1995, collapsing while running on the Island before going to work. His heart had simply stopped. June 15th was a Thursday. On Saturday morning I woke, saying to myself, “On the third day he rose again, and I’ll sell the Arizona house.” I like to believe that he was safe on the other side and was telling me so. I returned to Carefree the next year but it was not the same and so the house was sold. Manitou Island, to which we had moved year-around in 1973, became my home in both summer and winter.

title: The Hummingbird
summary: An observation triggers memory of a vignette from decades ago.
content:
One day sitting on the bedroom deck on the Island, I discovered a tiny hummingbird’s nest, exposed only because the leaves were off the trees. It was hanging about a foot away from the floor of the deck and only three feet from a planter that in the summer holds bright red geraniums. Easy living, I imagine, from a hummingbird’s point of view.

The tiny nest was a marvel, attached only to the fork of a tree twig by three plant fibers that had been looped over the twig and back into the nest proper. Probably originally lined with cottonwood down, it was now empty, swinging in the wind.

It reminded me of another nest brought down by a strong wind. When Ricky and Charley and I went out after the storm to look around, there it was, still with its soft interior *plus* a baby in the nest.

We took this extraordinary find indoors and I put it on a shelf near the kitchen sink. We were living in the original lake house at the time, the sink in a dark corner, the stove, with a large screen window over it, at the other side. Finding a medicine dropper and mixing a weak solution of honey and water I tried feeding it. Its long bill found the opening of the dropper without trouble and so the shelf became its home and I became its surrogate mother. The boys, very young, soon lost interest.

It was not long before the hummingbird — we never named it — recognized me as the food dispenser. It would chirp when I came into the kitchen. And so day-by-day it grew.

After two or three weeks went by I found it one morning on the edge of the nest, so, picking up nest and all, we went to the screen porch. I put the nest on a table and off the hummingbird fluttered. Off the table it went but could not gain altitude. I gently placed it back in its nest and took it back to its home, the shelf.

I can still see that little bird the next time I went into the kitchen. It was soaring in flight directly towards the window. It hit the screen and fell into an open can of bacon drippings standing next the stove. We fished it out and tried to clean it with detergent but of course it could not survive. That little thing had taken our hearts. If only I had had the wit to take the nest outdoors and leave it on the brick stair railing. It still feels like a death in the family.

title: The Junior League
summary: As a new college graduate, Molly was invited to join the Junior League. She thrived on the opportunity, developing her administrative skills and rising to become a regional director.
content:
The first Junior League was started in New York City in 1901 by college graduate homemakers who wished to use their educations in their community through volunteering in positions of responsibility.

The St. Paul League was incorporated in 1917. Its major efforts — there were a variety of other smaller projects — led to help in the establishment of Children’s Hospital, Merriam Park Community Center, the St. Paul Rehabilitation Center, and the Arts and Science Council, the latter leading to the first Science Museum building between Wabasha and Cedar.

On my return to St. Paul from college, I was invited to join the League. An intensive provisional course taught us the ins and outs and cooperative alliances of the various city agencies, public and private. My volunteer placement was at a nursery school where I learned that the best way to deal with an obstreperous youngster was to give him some responsibility.

After the intervening war years, I took up my membership again, this time working at such varied jobs as sorting stamps for the Historical Society and wringing out hot cloths for the physical therapist in the first year of the Rehabilitation Center.

Babysitters, however, were cheap and plentiful in those days, and so I began to take on the more responsible jobs in the League, sharing the leadership of the provisional course and then serving as treasurer. Each office was for a two-year term. In every case it was on-the-job training. You had been nominated by a committee that thought you could do it.

About that time our fifth child turned up. A member of the Nominating Committee of the League called and said, “I suppose you won’t be able to be president now.” “Oh, yes, I will,” I said. Those were the years when my administrative skills began to be developed. I’d always been the bossy type and my father had taught me double-entry bookkeeping. I’d also loved listening to his descriptions of his work and imagined that if I’d been a man I could have gone to work at FOK and been a success. So it was that as president I encouraged, delegated, hired and fired (we raised money through a Next-to New Shop), and performed in a follies singing show, another fundraiser.

Then came two years as regional director for the Association of Junior Leagues of America. I don’t remember the protocol by which I was nominated as a possible AJLA regional director, but I remember that since it would mean considerable time away from home, I felt that this was a matter for which I would need God’s guidance. I asked to be shown if it was right. I have always felt that the rightness was shown rather spectacularly.

The meeting of Region VII was held in Chicago with each League sending three or four delegates. I was chosen to sum up the two-day meeting. The extraordinary part is that I wrote that summation straight through without changing a word or a phrase — not common to me then or since. My St. Paul “handlers” sat with me and contributed the final *bon mot*. I was elected to a two-year term as the next regional director.

The AJLA Board met twice a year in New York City for ten days. We stayed in the Junior League headquarters in the Waldorf Astoria Hotel. Another meeting in a different part of the country preceded the annual conference to which all Leagues sent delegates. Mary, from Greenwich, Joan, from Montreal, Bea, from Tacoma, and I were elected the same year (1959) and became close friends. Besides our committee work and meetings as a whole when we were in New York City, we had time to go to the theater and/or out to dinner.

Committee work at that time was handled by mail, so I hired a part-time secretary. I also spent two days with each of the 16 Leagues in Region VII. The Region, as I recall, was comprised of Minnesota, Wisconsin, Illinois, Iowa, and Indiana. The by-laws and the budgets that I would read on the plane were enough to give me a clear picture of the League’s individual characteristics. Especially memorable were Chicago and Evanston, so close geographically and yet so dissimilar. I also made one trip to Texas to evaluate a new incoming League and discovered that the mores were so different in the South that I recommended that future evaluations be made by the director of the region.

Dick grinned and bore all my time away from home, and was an anchor for the children. There was also Anna, a peculiar sort but loving, who was the single caretaker. It was a heady time for me. I was the expert from out-of-town evaluating the Leagues in my area. And I was meeting and becoming friends with women from all over the country. I was also learning about nonprofit board-staff relationships and how they should be handled. Boards were to hire executive directors and set policy. Board members were to encourage and were not to micromanage. This would prove immensely valuable when I returned to volunteer work at home.

title: Obituary: The Rev. E.H.C. Mueller (Der Lutheraner)
summary: The following *Todesanzeige*, written *auf Deutsch* by the Rev. Herbert C. Mueller, Opa’s son, appeared on page 356 of *Der Lutheraner*, “The Official Organ of The Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod,” on October 26, 1948. An obituary *auf Englisch* also appeared in [*The Lutheran Witness*](https://www.frauerica.org/Text/OpaObit1.php) for October 5, 1948, on page 324.
content:
*P. Ernst H.C. Müller erblickte das Licht der Welt am 28 Juni 1874. Sein Geburtsort lieght in der Nähe von Kendallville, Ind., wo sein Vater, Wilhelm, damals Ackerbau betrieb. Seine Mutter Adelheid, geborne Rickmeyer, was ihrerzeit als Dichterin und Schriftstellerin tätig, ihren Lesern als Frau Erica bekannt.*

Pastor Ernst H.C. Mueller first saw the light of the world on June 28, 1874. His birthplace was in the vicinity of Kendallville, Indiana, where his father Wilhelm was then engaged in farming. His mother Adelheid, née Rickmeyer, was a poet and author in her time, known to her readers as Frau Erica.

*Der nun Heimgegangene wurde in der St. Johanneskirche in Forest Park, Ill., am 14 April 1889 konfirmiert. Die Familie war inzwischen umgezogen. Im Herbst deselben Jahres bezog er unser College in Milwaukee, um sich die nötige Vorbildung auf seine theologischen Studien anzueignen. 1895 trat er ins Seminar in St. Louis ein. Nach drei Jahren entließ ihn diese Anstalt ins Predigtamt. Er folge zunächst dem Ruf seines Herrn nach Arena Township bei Madison, Minn., an die dortige Zionsgemeinde.*

The dearly departed was confirmed in St. John’s church in Forest Park, Illinois, on April 14, 1889, where the family had just moved. In the fall of that year he was accepted to our college in Milwaukee so that he could make the necessary preparations for his theological studies. In 1895 he entered the seminary in St. Louis and three years later left that institution for the office of the ministry. He followed his Lord’s call to Arena Township near Madison, Minnesota, and the Zion congregation there.

*Er sammelte bald einige Lutheraner in Madison um Gottes Wort, und unter seiner Leitung entstand die dortige St. Johannesgemeinde.*

He soon gathered a few Lutherans in Madison around God’s Word, and under his leadership the St. John’s congregation was established.

*Wohl der Hauptausgabe seiner langen Amtstätigkeit erfüllte er als Seelsorger der Immanuelsgemeinde bei Janesville, Minn. Er stand ihr vor vom Jahre 1911 bis zu seinem Heimgang am 21 August. Fast bis Ende 1944 bediente er auch zwei Filialen, darunter die jetzige blühende Gemeinde in Waldorf, Minn. Der Entschlafene hatte immer ein Herz für das Kleinod der Gemeindeschule. Damit seine Gemeinde den Segen einer solchen Schule genießen möchte, brachte er während seiner langen Amtszeit manches Opfer, wofür ihm treue Gemeindeglieder viel Dank wissen werden.*

Of course, the greatest contribution of his ministerial career was as the spiritual care he provided near Janesville, Minnesota. He served there from 1911 until his death on August 21. Through 1944 he also served two branches of the congregation including the flourishing congregation in Waldorf, Minnesota. [Pastor Mueller] always had a place in his heart for the congregational school. When his congregation wanted to give its blessing to such a school, he made many sacrifices during his long ministry, for which many devoted members of the congregation made their thanks known.

*Man hatte genaue Pläne entworfen, am 22 August sein goldenes Amtsjubiläum feierlich zu begehen. Gott hatte aber eine himmlische Feier für seinen Diener vorgesehen: er rief ihn zu sich am Abend vor dem Jubiläum. Sein Tod war aber nicht grade unerwartet, zumal er seit August 1946 gekränkt hatte.*

Plans had been drawn up to celebrate the golden anniversary of his ministry. But God had provided a heavenly celebration for his servant: He called him to His side in the evening before the celebration. However, his death was not entirely unexpected, especially since he had been in ill health since August of 1946.

*Die Grablegung fand am 25 August statt. P. Carl J. Gundermann leitete im Familienkreis vormittags einen kurzen Trauergottesdeinst und amtierte später am Grab. Danach lag die sterbliche Hülle aufgebahrt unter der Kanzel, von der der Hingeschiedene manchem Trauernden Trost gespendet hatte. Prof. W.A. Pöhler, Anstaltspräes in St. Paul, hielt die Leichenrede, und P. H.J. Eggers versah den Altargottesdienst. Amtsbrüder standen Spalier zu Ehren ihres ältesten Mitarbeiters im südlichen Minnesota, während die Gemeindebeamten den verblichenen Körper zur letzten irdischen Ruhestätte auf dem Gottesacker unweit der Kirche hinaustrugen. Die Pastoren sangen auf dem Friedhof das Lied: “O wie selig seid ihr doch, ihr Frommen.”*

The burial took place on August 25. P[astor] Carl J. Gundermann led a short service for the family in the morning and later officiated at the grave. After the family ceremony, the mortal remains were laid out before the chancel, from which the deceased had given so many mourners comfort. Prof. W.A. Poehler, college president in St. Paul, delivered the funeral sermon, and Pastor H.J. Eggers served as liturgist. Ministerial brethren formed a line of honor for their seniormost colleague in southern Minnesota while officers of the congregation bore the body to its final resting place in the nearby cemetery. In the churchyard, the pastors sang the hymn “O How Blessed You Are, You Faithful.”

*Am 12 April 1899 ehelichte er Fräulein Helene Hedder aus Chicago. Seine treue Gattin, die volle 49 Jahre Freud und Leid mit ihm geteilt hatte, überlebt ihn sowie vier Söhne und vier Töchter. Ein Söhnchen ging dem Vater in der zarten Kindheit in die Ewigkeit voraus. Der älteste und der jüngste Sohn [unterzeichneter] stehen im Predigtamt. Drei der Töchter dienten einige Jahre lang als Gemeindeschullehrerinnen im Weinberg des Herrn. Der Heimgeschiedene hinterläßt auch fünf Schwestern nebst einem Bruder.*

On April 12, 1899, he married Miss Helen Hedder of Chicago. His true life partner, who shared joy and sorrow with him for 49 years, survives him as do four sons and four daughters. A son entered eternal life in tender childhood before his father. His eldest and youngest [subscribed] sons are in the ministerial office. Three of his daughters served several years as parish schoolteachers in the Lord’s vineyard. The departed also leaves five sisters and a brother.

*“Der Tod seiner Heiligen ist wert gehalten vor dem Herrn,” Psalm 116, 15.*

“Precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of his saints.” Psalm 116:15

title: Paul Gerhardt Mueller, Jr.
content:
Reverend Paul Gerhardt Mueller went to his eternal rest on Wednesday, November 12, 2025, at The Waterford at Wisconsin Rapids at the age of 87. He was born in Tri Mountain, MI, on May 3, 1938, to the late Rev. Paul G and Ellen E. Mueller.

Paul graduated from Concordia Junior College in St. Paul Minnesota. Concordia Senior College in Fort Wayne, IN, and Concordia Seminary in St. Louis, MO. He served St. Paul Lutheran Church in Bennett, IA, where he met his future wife, Sandra Hein. They were married May 28, 1972, and were blessed with two children, Tara Mueller of Westmoreland, TN, and Karl Mueller of Menasha, WI.

Paul also served at St. Paul’s Kankakee, IL, Christ of the Bay in Green Bay, WI, and St. John in Wisconsin Rapids until his retirement in 2003. While at Kankakee he served as secretary for the Kankakee Pastors Conference. In Wisconsin Rapids he chaired the Cranberry Country Clergy for several years and also served as treasurer for the North Wisconsin District Pastors Conference for a number of years.

After his retirement Paul and Sandra spent winters in Florida and much of the summer at their cabin on Pickerel Lake, WI. It was there that Paul taught his family how to fish and appreciate God’s creations. Paul also worked part-time as a tax preparer for H&R Block, and he did seasonal work taking orders for FIGI’s in Stevens Point. He did pulpit supply for local churches and served as a visitor for the homebound at Immanuel Lutheran Church in Wisconsin Rapids.

One of Paul’s greatest joys was having his granddaughters, Alexandra and Morgan, visit during the summer. The families would go to the cabin, take short trips, work with flowers and take walks with some serious discussions.

Paul was a big fan of the Green Bay Packers and the St. Louis Cardinals. He was known for having a shorter Sunday service if the Packers had a noon game.

Survivors include his wife of 53 years Sandra, his children Tara (Jerome Hooge) Mueller and Karl Mueller; his granddaughters, Alexandra Maddox and Morgan Rye; his sister Erika (Robert) Robeson, brother-in-law Dale (Kathy) Hein, sisters-in-law Cindy (John) Gibson and Twyla (Kim Cross) Hein.

He was preceded in death by his parents and his sister Esther Mueller.

A memorial Service will be held at a later date.

The family would like to thank the staff of the Waterford at Wisconsin Rapids and Heartland Hospice for their wonderful care and concern.

title: Obituary: Paul Henry Jacob Feiertag
content:
Paul Henry Jacob Feiertag died on July 2, 2024, in Everett, Washington. He was born in Cheyenne Wells, Colorado, on September 8, 1936, as the second of four siblings born to John and Martha Feiertag. He served in the U.S. Army, in an armory division in West Germany. He married Ingrid Feiertag on July 3, 1965, and served as principal and elementary school teacher at Holy Cross Lutheran School in San Antonio, Texas, before returning to Concordia Seminary in St. Louis, Missouri, to complete a Master of Divinity in 1972. He was the fourth generation in his family to serve as a Lutheran pastor, serving four churches in Arkansas and Missouri and as a hospice chaplain at the end of his career. While living in Santa Rosa, California, he and Ingrid became strong advocates for LGBTQIA+ people and their families in church and society.

As dementia began to take its toll on him, he and Ingrid, along with his oldest sister Gloria relocated to Everett, Washington, to have local support from their younger son.

His first job was manually pumping a church organ for his father. He worked at a gas station, did food service, drove tanks for the Army, helped his future wife learn to play piano, was a respectable Bible scholar, maintained his own cars, helped build houses, marched in pride parades, accompanied people who were dying, facilitated Bible studies, fixed many household items, eventually learned to dance the polka, could sing the melody, and was an effective and moving preacher. He was a loving husband and dad. He navigated Apple computers pretty well, loved to eat popcorn, and really, really liked collecting rocks.

Paul was a hard-working, generous person. He had a warm sense of humor. He was not afraid to do the right thing, even if that meant personal difficulty, sacrifice, or risk, including welcoming the first African American family into the parochial school in San Antonio despite being instructed not to and ensuring his son Dan had the opportunity to learn scouting skills from an African American Scout Master.

Paul is survived by his wife Ingrid, sons Dan (Liz Lemke) and Tim, and sister Gloria. A memorial service will be held on Saturday, August 31, at 2 p.m. at Trinity Lutheran Church, 2324 Lombard Avenue in Everett, Washington, with available livestream link. In lieu of flowers, contributions can be made to Trinity Lutheran in Everett or to the charity of your choice.

title: Obituary: Peter Albert Crawley
summary: Peter’s parents lived in Hamden, Connecticut, about five miles north of New Haven. He was returning to Rhode Island after visiting his father, who had been hospitalized in New Haven.
content:
**Peter Albert Crawley** ’71 A.M., Tiverton, R.I., a personnel management specialist at the Naval Underwater Systems Center in Newport, R.I.: Aug. 19, in an automobile crash on the Newport Bridge. Mr. Crawley graduated from Muhlenberg College in Pennsylvania in 1969. He served in Vietnam as a chaplain’s assistant and for a time was a budget analyst with the U.S. Department of Agriculture in Washington, D.C. Survivors include his wife, Allison McMillan Crawley ’74, 24 Lowell St., Tiverton 02878, co-director of Brown’s Alumni and Development Information Services; a son, Daniel; and a daughter, Susan, who was born five days after Mr. Crawley’s death. His father is Willard B. Crawley ’28.

NEWPORT, R.I. - One man died and four people were Injured in a three-car accident on Newport Bridge Friday night in which one car plummeted 100 feet into Narragansett Bay. State police said a car driven by Peter Crawley, 35, of Tiverton, was heading east on the bridge when it struck a car going west, bounced off the guardrail and went end-over-end into the bay. Crawley, pulled from the waters about an hour after the 8:30 p.m. accident, was taken to Newport Hospital where he died shortly before 11 p.m.

State Police Capt. John Devine said he believed speed wasn’t a factor. “His car and the other car just struck at the right angle to catapult him over the other vehicle and into the water. I think it’s just a freak accident,” Devine said. One of four women in the car Crawley struck was admitted to Newport Hospital and listed in good condition. She was identified as Florence Morse, 67, of Waterford, Conn. The others, who were released after treatment, were identified as Alma Johnson, 65, also of Waterford, and Doris Douglas, 68, and Deborah Brown, 24, both of Jamestown. An eastbound car behind Crawley’s veered to the right and grazed the guardrail, a witness at the scene said. Coast Guard divers made a search of the water for other possible victims, thinking there might have been passengers in Crawley’s car. The search ended when Crawley was located. For several hours, traffic was backed up for miles and police were alternating eastbound and westbound traffic on one lane on the 2.5-mile-long bridge. By midnight, traffic on the span had returned to normal.

title: Alfred Martin Rehwinkel
content:
Alfred Martin Rehwinkel was born 25 June 1887 in a log cabin parsonage on the northern frontier of Wisconsin in Marathon County, the son of Pastor W. and Henrietta (nee Zernikow) Rehwinkel. He was baptized on 10 July 1887 at Zion Lutheran Church (Town of Maine, Marathon County, Wisconsin).

He received his college and professional education at Concordia College (Milwaukee, Wisconsin); Concordia Seminary (Saint Louis, Missouri) (graduated 1910); and at several theological schools and universities in Canada and the United States. He earned B.A. (1916), B.D. (1918) and M.A. (1919) degrees from the University of Alberta at Edmonton, Alberta.

Rehwinkel was ordained into the holy ministry in 1910. His early service was on the frontier of western Canada, at first as a traveling missionary in southern Alberta and British Columbia, then as a pastor in Edmonton, Alberta.

Rehwinkel was the first president of the Alberta and British Columbia Conference of The Lutheran Church—Missouri Synod (LCMS) and led in the organization of the first district of the synod in western Canada. He was also one of the founders of Concordia College, Edmonton, and first president of its board of control. Later he served for seven years as a professor at the school.

Rehwinkel became president of Saint John’s College, Winfield, Kansas, where he served for eight years. In 1936 he was called to a professorship in systematic and practical theology at Concordia Seminary, Saint Louis. He retired in 1965.

While in Canada, Rehwinkel was active in public life, serving on several important national and civic boards and committees. He was a member of the board of the public library, the board of directors of the Edmonton Art Association and the National Diamond Jubilee Committee in 1927. His German translation of the Canadian national anthem, “Oh, Canada!” was widely used in German circles in Canada.

Rehwinkel is the author of the following books: The World Today—A Challenge to the Christian Church; Communism and the Church; The Flood; The Voice of Conscience; Planned Parenthood and Birth Control in the Light of Christian Ethics; Dr. Bessie, the life story and romance of a pioneer doctor on the western Canadian frontier; The Age of the Earth; and Chronology of the Bible.

Rehwinkel was a regular lecturer for Lutheran Laymen’s League seminars, beginning in 1943. The first of these was held in Fort Wayne, Indiana, on 19 February 1943.

In 1956 Concordia Seminary granted Rehwinkel a sabbatical leave of absence for one semester. He spent the six months traveling and studying abroad. He visited the Lutheran Church and seminary in Australia served as guest lecturer at the LCMS mission seminary in India and in other mission locations in that country. He also spent several weeks in Bible lands, namely, ancient Babylon, Assyria, Mesopotamia, Syria, Phoenicia, Palestine, Egypt, Greece and Italy and then spent some time in Germany and other European countries.

In 1962 and 1963 Rehwinkel was invited to serve as an exchange professor at the Lutheran seminary in Porto Alegre, Brazil, where he served for one semester and then traveled extensively throughout the length and breadth of Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay and Chile. In 1964-1965 he spent another ten months abroad, serving as an exchange professor at the Lutheran seminary in Adelaide, South Australia. At that time, he traveled extensively in Australia. He was then invited to visit New Guinea and serve as lecturer for the LCMS missionary conference on that island. While there he visited the mission stations of the LCMS and other churches in that part of the world.

From New Guinea, Rehwinkel went to Africa and spent three months traveling a total of 18,000 miles on that continent. He again visited many mission stations and wildlife parks and refuges. He became particularly interested in the racial problems confronting southern Africa in the 1960s. In 1966 he traveled extensively in Mexico, and in 1967 on his 80th birthday he was the speaker at a mass celebration of 3,000 people for the Canadian Centennial in Edmonton.

Rehwinkel was married to Bessie Lee Efner, a medical doctor, in 1912. They had three children: daughters Dorothy and Helen and a son, Pastor Eugene Rehwinkel. His wife died on 26 May 1962. See Alfred M. Rehwinkel, Dr. Bessie: The Life Story and Romance of a Pioneer Lady Doctor on Our Western and the Canadian Frontier as Told by Herself and Here Presented in a Running Narrative by Her Husband (Saint Louis: Concordia Publishing House, 1963).

title: Obituary: Richard William Gahl
summary: Dick Gahl and Joel Nickel, classmates at Concordia Seminary in St. Louis, shared a Mueller heritage. Dick’s grandmother Emma, daughter of Enno Mueller, and Joel’s grandfather Ernst, son of Wilhelm Mueller, were cousins.
content:
Richard William Gahl died on May 14, 2021, after complications from cancer. Born in Chicago in 1939, he attended Lutheran schools and graduated from Concordia Seminary in St. Louis, Missouri in 1965. He was ordained in the Lutheran Church Missouri Synod (LCMS) that year.

After serving congregations in Indiana and Pennsylvania, he was appointed executive director of the Ohio District, LCMS, located in Olmsted Falls, Ohio, where he served for nearly 25 years. He retired in 2004 and served in various volunteer capacities, including as board chair at Lutheran Metropolitan Ministries in Cleveland, Ohio. In his retirement, he also continued to write and study, always seeking to deepen his understanding of the scriptures.

He was an avid Indians baseball fan, a voracious reader, and an incorrigible hunter of lost golf balls. He loved music, having studied piano and organ, and in his later years enjoyed singing with Oberlin College’s Musical Union.

He is survived by his wife of 54 years Judith A. Gahl, his brother John Gahl and his wife, Connie, and his two children, Karen and David, and their families.

title:
Obituary  
Robert Kent Mueller
summary: Robert Kent Mueller, the fifth child of Herbert and Ruth (Polley) Mueller, was a composer, orchestral conductor, organist and veteran music professor at the University of Arkansas.
content:
{{image:901}}

Robert Kent Mueller, age 66, of Fayetteville, Ark., passed away on Thursday, March 6, 2025. Bob was born May 22, 1958, in Hankinson, N.D. He was preceded in death by his parents, Herbert Christian Mueller and Ruth Polley Mueller, and by his brother, Herb Mueller.

Bob graduated from Western High School in Bay City, Mich. He attended Northern Michigan University, where he earned a Bachelor of Science in Music (piano) degree, with minors in Meteorology and German. At NMU, Bob received the Outstanding Pianist Award, and was later honored with an Outstanding Alumni Award. Bob earned a Master of Music in Composition degree, with an additional concentration in Orchestral Conducting from Bowling Green State University. He received his Doctor of Musical Arts degree in Music Composition from the University of Cincinnati College-Conservatory of Music.

Bob joined the faculty of the University of Arkansas in 1988, where he conducted the University Symphony Orchestra and taught Music Composition and Music Theory. Over the course of his 37-year career as a professor, Bob touched the lives of thousands of students. He impacted hundreds more through his summer position at the Interlochen Center for the Arts in Interlochen, Michigan, where he taught Composition and Theory for 15 summers. He was a gifted teacher who found great satisfaction in helping his students progress as performers, conductors, educators, and composers.

Bob was an accomplished composer, writing prolifically in many genres, including full symphonic programmatic works, vocal and instrumental chamber music, and sacred works. His music was performed by orchestras nationwide, including the Cincinnati, Omaha, Fort Smith, Billings, Bozeman, Jackson (MS), Lansing, Arkansas, and North Arkansas Symphonies. At the University of Arkansas, Bob's works were performed by the University Symphony Orchestra, the Wind Ensemble, the Percussion Ensemble, the Lyrique Quintette, and the Arkansas Brassworks. He received numerous commissions, and was widely published and performed. He produced three chamber music albums, most recently Dream Gardens, on the MSR Classics label.

Bob was composer-in-residence three times for the Fort Smith Symphony. He also served as guest composer-in-residence for several universities. He was the recipient of numerous awards from the American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers (ASCAP).
